questions.question: Which of the following statements with respect to Endoplasmic Reticulum is incorrect?

options.A: SER are the sites for lipid synthesis
options.B: RER has ribosomes attached to ER
options.D: In prokaryotes only RER are present

questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: D

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Regarding Meiosis, which of the statements is incorrect?

options.A: Four haploid cells are formed at the end of Meiosis-II
options.B: There are two stages in Meiosis, Meiosis-I and II
options.C: DNA replication occurs in S phase of Meiosis-II
options.D: Pairing of homologous chromosomes and recombination occurs in Meiosis-I

questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: C

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statements are true for spermatogenesis but do not hold true for Oogenesis? (a) It results in the formation of haploid gametes (b) Differentiation of gamete occurs after the completion of meiosis (c) Meiosis occurs continuously in a mitotically dividing stem cell population (d) It is controlled by the Luteinising hormone (LH) and Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH) secreted by the anterior pituitary (e) It is initiated at puberty
questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: A

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Select the incorrect statement with reference to mitosis:

options.A: Splitting of centromere occurs at anaphase
options.B: All the chromosomes lie at the equator at metaphase
options.C: Spindle fibres attach to centromere of chromosomes

questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: C

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Select the incorrect statement with respect to acquired immunity.

options.A: Acquired immunity is non-specific type of defense present at the time of birth.
options.B: Primary response is produced when our body encounters a pathogen for the first time.
options.C: Anamnestic response is elicited on subsequent encounters with the same pathogen.
options.D: Anamnestic response is due to memory of first encounter.

questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: A

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ten E.coli cells with 15N - dsDNA are incubated in medium containg 14N nucleotide. After 60 minutes, how many E.coli cells will have DNA totally free from 15N?
questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: D

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Select the incorrect statement regarding synapses:

options.A: Impulse transmission across a chemical synapse is always faster than that across an electrical synapse.
options.B: The membranes of presynaptic and postsynaptic neurons are in close proximity in an electrical synapse.
options.C: Electrical current can flow directly from one neuron into the other across the electrical synapse.

questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: A

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Water falls from a height of 60 m at the rate of 15 kg/s to operate a turbine. The losses due to frictional force are 10% of the input energy. How much power is generated by the turbine? (g=10 m/s2)
questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: C

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Identify the incorrect statement.

options.A: Heart wood does not conduct water but gives mechanical support
options.B: Sapwood is involved in conduction of water and minerals from root to leaf
options.C: Sapwood is the innermost secondary xylem and is lighter in colour
options.D: Due to deposition of tannins, resins, oils etc., heart wood is dark in colour

questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: C

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statements is not correct?

options.A: In man insulin is synthesised as a proinsulin
options.B: The proinsulin has an extra peptide called C-peptide.
options.C: The functional insulin has A and B chains linked together by hydrogen bonds.
options.D: Genetically engineered insulin is produced in E.Coli.

questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: C

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: If the distance between two consecutive base pairs is 0.34 nm and the total number of base pairs of a DNA double helix in a typical mammalian cell is 6.6×10⁹ bp, then the length of the DNA is approximately
questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: C

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is not correct about carbon monoxide?

options.B: It reduces oxygen carrying ability of blood.
options.C: The carboxyhaemoglobin (haemoglobin bound to CO) is less stable than oxyhaemoglobin.
options.D: It is produced due to incomplete combustion.

questions.explanation: Official NEET answer: C

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following microbes is NOT correctly matched with its product?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Lactobacillus → lactic acid. Acetic acid → Acetobacter aceti.
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.1
questions.ncert_line: Acetic acid (vinegar) is produced by Acetobacter aceti, not Lactobacillus. Lactobacillus produces lactic acid.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Loop of Henle is INCORRECT?

options.A: Descending limb is permeable to water
options.B: Ascending limb is impermeable to water
options.C: It helps in concentration of urine
options.D: It is found in all nephrons equally

questions.explanation: Correct: D. Long loop of Henle only in juxtamedullary nephrons, not all nephrons equally.
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products and their Elimination, Section 19.3
questions.ncert_line: Loop of Henle is long only in juxtamedullary nephrons (deep in medulla). Cortical nephrons have short loops.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a dihybrid cross (AaBb × AaBb), the phenotypic ratio is 9:3:1. The number of plants showing both recessive traits is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. aabb = 1 out of 16. Students often confuse with the '3' groups.
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.1
questions.ncert_line: 9:3:1 ratio. The '1' represents aabb (both recessive) = 1/16 of offspring.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT true about the genetic code?

options.B: It is degenerate (multiple codons for one amino acid)
options.D: It is non-ambiguous (one codon = one amino acid)

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Genetic code is NON-overlapping. It IS universal, degenerate, and non-ambiguous.
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.5
questions.ncert_line: The genetic code is non-overlapping — each nucleotide belongs to only one codon. It was thought to be overlapping but is not.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium is disturbed by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Random mating is a CONDITION for H-W equilibrium (not a disruptive force).
questions.ncert_line: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires random mating. It is disrupted by selection, genetic drift, mutation, migration, non-random mating.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ethylene gas promotes all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ethylene does NOT promote apical dominance — that's auxin. Ethylene promotes senescence and ripening.
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.5
questions.ncert_line: Ethylene promotes fruit ripening, leaf abscission, senescence and breaking of seed dormancy. Apical dominance is promoted by auxin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which type of sexual reproduction do two gametes that are flagellated and similar in size fuse together?
questions.explanation: Isogamy involves fusion of two gametes that are flagellated and equal in size � as seen in Ulothrix. Anisogamy involves gametes that are dissimilar in size. Oogamy involves a large non-motile female gamete and a small motile male gamete. Vegetative reproduction occurs by fragmentation, not gamete fusion.
questions.ncert_line: These gametes can be flagellated and similar in size (as in Ulothrix)...Such reproduction is called isogamous.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following algae shows oogamous sexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Volvox shows oogamy � fusion of a large non-motile female gamete with a small motile male gamete. Spirogyra shows isogamy with non-flagellated but equal-sized gametes. Ulothrix shows isogamy with flagellated equal-sized gametes. Eudorina shows anisogamy where gametes differ in size.
questions.ncert_line: Fusion between one large, non-motile (static) female gamete and a smaller, motile male gamete is termed oogamous, e.g., Volvox, Fucus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The natural classification system for flowering plants, based on both external and internal features, was given by which scientists?

options.A: George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker

questions.explanation: George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker gave the natural classification for flowering plants, considering both external and internal features including anatomy, embryology and phytochemistry. Linnaeus gave an artificial system based on androecium structure. Whittaker proposed the Five Kingdom classification. Aristotle and Theophrastus were ancient Greek scholars not linked to this natural classification system.
questions.ncert_line: Such a classification for flowering plants was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which pigment is responsible for the characteristic colour of red algae?
questions.explanation: r-Phycoerythrin is the red pigment that dominates in Rhodophyceae, giving red algae their characteristic colour � directly stated in NCERT. Option A (fucoxanthin) is a xanthophyll pigment found in brown algae (Phaeophyceae), not red algae. Option C (chlorophyll b) is the accessory pigment found in green algae (Chlorophyceae). Option D (carotenoids) are present in brown algae along with chlorophyll a and c, but do not define red algae.
questions.ncert_line: The members of rhodophyceae are commonly called red algae because of the predominance of the red pigment, r-phycoerythrin in their body.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which organelle are pyrenoids located in green algae, and what do they contain?

options.A: Nucleus; they contain DNA and RNA
options.B: Vacuole; they contain mannitol and laminarin
options.C: Chloroplast; they contain protein and starch
options.D: Cell wall; they contain cellulose and pectose

questions.explanation: Pyrenoids are storage bodies found inside chloroplasts of green algae, and they contain protein and starch � this is directly stated in the NCERT text. Option A is wrong because pyrenoids are not found in the nucleus and do not contain DNA or RNA. Option B is wrong because mannitol and laminarin are food storage compounds found in brown algae, not inside pyrenoids. Option D is wrong because the cell wall of green algae is made of cellulose and pectose, but pyrenoids are not located there.
questions.ncert_line: Most of the members have one or more storage bodies called pyrenoids located in the chloroplasts. Pyrenoids contain protein besides starch.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where does glycolysis occur in the cell?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm of the cell. Option A is wrong because the mitochondrial matrix is the site of the Krebs cycle, not glycolysis. Option C is wrong because the nucleus is involved in genetic material storage and transcription, not glycolysis. Option D is wrong because the endoplasmic reticulum is involved in protein and lipid synthesis, not glucose breakdown.
questions.ncert_line: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm of the cell and is present in all living organisms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In plants, the glucose used in glycolysis is mainly derived from which molecule?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that in plants, glucose for glycolysis is derived from sucrose, which is the end product of photosynthesis. Sucrose is converted into glucose and fructose by the enzyme invertase, and these enter the glycolytic pathway. Option A is wrong because starch is a storage carbohydrate and is a secondary source, not the primary one mentioned in the text. Option B is wrong because cellulose is a structural carbohydrate and is not broken down for glycolysis. Option D is wrong because maltose is not mentioned in the NCERT text as a glucose source for the glycolytic pathway in plants.
questions.ncert_line: In plants, this glucose is derived from sucrose, which is the end product of photosynthesis, or from storage carbohydrates.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which enzyme is responsible for phosphorylating glucose and fructose to give rise to glucose-6-phosphate?
questions.explanation: Hexokinase is the enzyme that phosphorylates glucose (and fructose) to produce glucose-6-phosphate in the first step of glycolysis. However, it should be noted that when fructose is phosphorylated, it typically gives fructose-6-phosphate, not glucose-6-phosphate directly. The NCERT text does state that hexokinase phosphorylates glucose and fructose to give glucose-6-phosphate as part of glycolysis. Option A is wrong because invertase converts sucrose into glucose and fructose. Option B is wrong because isomerase converts glucose-6-phosphate to fructose-6-phosphate, a later step in glycolysis. Option D is wrong because phosphorylase is associated with glycogen breakdown, not this reaction.
questions.ncert_line: Glucose and fructose are phosphorylated to give rise to glucose-6-phosphate by the activity of the enzyme hexokinase.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which enzymes catalyse the conversion of pyruvic acid to CO₂ and ethanol during alcoholic fermentation in yeast?

options.A: Pyruvic acid decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase
options.B: Lactate dehydrogenase and pyruvic acid decarboxylase

questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that pyruvic acid decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase together catalyse the conversion of pyruvic acid to CO₂ and ethanol in yeast. Pyruvic acid decarboxylase converts pyruvate to acetaldehyde and CO₂, while alcohol dehydrogenase reduces acetaldehyde to ethanol using NADH. Option B is wrong because lactate dehydrogenase is involved in lactic acid production, not alcoholic fermentation. Option C is wrong because citrate synthase is an enzyme of the Krebs cycle, not fermentation. Option D is wrong because pyruvic dehydrogenase works in aerobic respiration inside mitochondria, and lactate dehydrogenase is specific to lactic acid fermentation.
questions.ncert_line: The enzymes, pyruvic acid decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase catalyse these reactions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where does the complete oxidation of pyruvate take place during aerobic respiration in eukaryotes?

options.A: In the cytoplasm of the cell
options.B: On the outer membrane of the mitochondria
options.C: In the matrix of the mitochondria
options.D: In the nucleus of the cell

questions.explanation: The complete oxidation of pyruvate takes place in the matrix of the mitochondria. Pyruvate is first transported from the cytoplasm (where glycolysis occurs) into the mitochondrial matrix, where it undergoes oxidative decarboxylation and then enters the Krebs cycle (TCA cycle) for complete oxidation. Option A is wrong because glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm, but complete oxidation of pyruvate does not. Option B is wrong because the outer membrane is not the site of pyruvate oxidation; the inner membrane is associated with the electron transport chain and ATP synthesis. Option D is wrong because the nucleus is involved in genetic functions, not cellular respiration.
questions.ncert_line: What is interesting to note is that the first process takes place in the matrix of the mitochondria while the second process is located on the inner membrane of the mitochondria.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), reproductive health means total well-being in which aspects of reproduction?

options.B: Physical, emotional, behavioural and social aspects

questions.explanation: WHO defines reproductive health as total well-being in physical, emotional, behavioural and social aspects — all four are required. Option A is wrong because it leaves out behavioural and social aspects. Option C incorrectly adds 'financial' which is never mentioned in NCERT. Option D is wrong because it omits the physical aspect and incorrectly adds 'cultural'.
questions.ncert_line: According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), reproductive health means a total well-being in all aspects of reproduction, i.e., physical, emotional, behavioural and social.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: India's family planning programmes were first initiated at the national level in which year?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that family planning programmes were initiated in 1951. Option A (1947) is the year of Indian independence, not the start of family planning. Option B (1956) corresponds to no relevant event in this context. Option D (1961) is a plausible but incorrect year — the programmes had already begun a decade earlier.
questions.ncert_line: These programmes called 'family planning' were initiated in 1951 and were periodically assed over the past decades.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Amniocentesis is a procedure in which amniotic fluid of the developing foetus is analysed. Which of the following genetic disorders can be tested using this procedure?

options.A: Diabetes mellitus, colour blindness and albinism
options.C: Down syndrome, haemophilia and sickle-cell anaemia
options.D: Klinefelter's syndrome, cystic fibrosis and night blindness

questions.explanation: NCERT specifically names Down syndrome, haemophilia and sickle-cell anaemia as examples of genetic disorders that can be tested through amniocentesis. The amniotic fluid contains foetal cells whose chromosomes and genes can be analysed to detect chromosomal abnormalities (like Down syndrome), sex-linked disorders (like haemophilia), and single-gene disorders (like sickle-cell anaemia). Option A includes diabetes mellitus and colour blindness which are not typically diagnosed via amniocentesis as described in NCERT. Option B and D contain disorders not cited in the NCERT context for amniocentesis testing.
questions.ncert_line: This procedure is used to test for the presence of certain genetic disorders such as, down syndrome, haemophilia, sickle-cell anemia, etc., determine the survivability of the foetus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The lactational amenorrhea method of contraception is effective for a maximum period of how long following parturition, according to NCERT?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that the lactational amenorrhea method is effective only up to a maximum of six months after parturition. Option A (three months) is too short and not mentioned in the text. Option C (nine months) and Option D (twelve months) are longer durations that are also not supported by the NCERT text.
questions.ncert_line: this method has been reported to be effective only upto a maximum period of six months following parturition.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During which days of the menstrual cycle is the fertile period when ovulation is expected, making fertilisation most likely?
questions.explanation: NCERT specifically states that the fertile period is from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle, as ovulation is expected during this time and chances of fertilisation are very high. Periodic abstinence as a contraceptive method involves avoiding coitus during this window. Option A (day 1 to 7) corresponds to the menstrual phase, not the fertile period. Option B (day 5 to 12) is partially overlapping but incorrect as per NCERT. Option D (day 18 to 28) is after ovulation has already occurred, making fertilisation less likely.
questions.ncert_line: Periodic abstinence is one such method in which the couples avoid or abstain from coitus from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle when ovulation could be expected.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In grasses, what is the shape of guard cells?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that in grasses, guard cells are dumb-bell shaped. Option A (bean-shaped) is the shape of guard cells in most other (dicot) plants, not grasses — a common confusion in NEET. Option C (spherical) is not mentioned anywhere for guard cells. Option D (elongated cylindrical) is a made-up description not found in NCERT.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In grasses, the guard cells are dumb-bell shaped.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of vascular bundle is found in dicotyledonous stems because cambium is present between xylem and phloem?
questions.explanation: Open vascular bundles have cambium between xylem and phloem and can form secondary tissues — this is the feature of dicotyledonous stems as stated in NCERT. Option A (closed) is wrong because closed bundles lack cambium and are found in monocots. Option B (radial) is wrong because radial refers to the arrangement of xylem and phloem on alternate radii, as seen in roots. Option D (conjoint) refers to xylem and phloem situated along the same radius, not specifically to the presence of cambium.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In dicotyledonous stems, cambium is present between phloem and xylem. Such vascular bundles because of the presence of cambium possess the ability to form secondary xylem and phloem tissues, and hence are called open vascular bundles.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the role of trichomes found on the stem of plants?

options.A: Absorption of water and minerals from soil
options.B: Regulation of stomatal opening and closing
options.C: Prevention of water loss due to transpiration
options.D: Conduction of food materials through the plant

questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that trichomes help prevent water loss due to transpiration. Option A is wrong because absorption of water and minerals is the function of root hairs, not trichomes. Option B is wrong because opening and closing of stomata is regulated by guard cells, not trichomes. Option D is wrong because conduction of food is the function of phloem, a vascular tissue, not trichomes.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The trichomes help in preventing water loss due to transpiration.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a monocotyledonous stem, where are the vascular bundles located?

options.A: Arranged in a ring just inside the endodermis
options.C: Arranged in two distinct rings near the pith
options.D: Clustered only at the centre of the stem

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that in a monocot stem, vascular bundles are scattered throughout the ground tissue. Option A describes the ring arrangement, which is the characteristic of a dicot stem, not a monocot stem. Option C is incorrect because monocot vascular bundles do not form two distinct rings. Option D is wrong because the bundles are not clustered only at the centre — peripheral bundles are generally smaller but present throughout.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The monocot stem has a sclerenchymatous hypodermis, a large number of scattered vascular bundles, each surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheath, and a large, conspicuous parenchymatous ground tissue.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which cells in the pericycle of a dicotyledonous root are responsible for the initiation of lateral roots?

options.A: Thin-walled parenchymatous cells of the pericycle
options.B: Barrel-shaped endodermal cells with casparian strips
options.C: Conjunctive tissue cells lying between xylem and phloem
options.D: Collenchymatous cells present below the epidermis

questions.explanation: NCERT states that the pericycle in dicotyledonous roots consists of parenchymatous cells (thin-walled, not thick-walled as incorrectly cited). These cells are responsible for the initiation of lateral roots and also participate in vascular cambium formation during secondary growth. Option B is wrong because barrel-shaped endodermal cells with Casparian strips regulate the apoplastic movement of water and minerals, not lateral root initiation. Option C is wrong because conjunctive tissue lies between xylem and phloem bundles and does not initiate lateral roots. Option D describes collenchyma found in the hypodermis of dicot stems, which is unrelated to root anatomy. Note: The NCERT line cited in the question incorrectly describes pericycle cells as 'thick-walled'; NCERT actually describes them as thin-walled parenchymatous cells.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: Next to endodermis lies a few layers of thick-walled parenchymatous cells referred to as pericycle. Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium during the secondary growth takes place in these cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which portal system carries hypothalamic hormones to the anterior pituitary gland?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that hypothalamic hormones travel through a portal circulatory system to reach the anterior pituitary. The hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system carries releasing and inhibiting hormones from the hypothalamus to the anterior pituitary gland, where they regulate secretion of various tropic hormones. Option B (lymphatic) is wrong because lymphatic vessels carry lymph, not these hormones. Option C (coronary) is wrong because coronary circulation supplies the heart. Option D (systemic venous) is wrong because that is the general return circulation and is not specifically named here.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: These hormones reach the pituitary gland through a portal circulatory system and regulate the functions of the anterior pituitary.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which part of the pituitary gland produces Melanocyte Stimulating Hormone (MSH)?
questions.explanation: MSH is produced by the pars intermedia of the pituitary gland. NCERT states: 'Pars intermedia secretes only one hormone called melanocyte stimulating hormone (MSH).' However, the reasoning for rejecting Option D needs correction: pars intermedia is actually a part of adenohypophysis (along with pars distalis and pars tuberalis). So technically, Option D (adenohypophysis) could also be considered correct as a broader category. But since the question asks for the specific part, Option A (pars intermedia) is the most precise and correct answer. Option B (pars distalis) produces GH, PRL, TSH, ACTH, LH, and FSH. Option C (pars nervosa) is part of neurohypophysis and stores/releases oxytocin and ADH (vasopressin). Note: In humans, pars intermedia is almost vestigial, but NCERT explicitly attributes MSH secretion to it.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: Pars intermedia secretes only one hormone called melanocyte stimulating hormone (MSH).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Oxytocin and vasopressin are synthesised by which structure and stored in which part of the pituitary?

options.A: Synthesised by hypothalamus; stored in neurohypophysis
options.B: Synthesised by pars distalis; stored in neurohypophysis
options.C: Synthesised by neurohypophysis; stored in adenohypophysis
options.D: Synthesised by hypothalamus; stored in adenohypophysis

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that oxytocin and vasopressin are synthesised by the hypothalamus and transported axonally to the neurohypophysis where they are stored and released. Option B is wrong because pars distalis does not synthesise these hormones. Option C is wrong because neurohypophysis stores them but does not synthesise them. Option D is wrong because adenohypophysis is not the storage site — neurohypophysis is.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: Neurohypophysis (pars nervosa) also known as posterior pituitary, stores and releases two hormones called oxytocin and vasopressin, which are actually synthesised by the hypothalamus and are transported axonally to neurohypophysis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which hormone stimulates vigorous contraction of the uterus at the time of childbirth?
questions.explanation: Oxytocin acts on smooth muscles and specifically stimulates strong uterine contractions during childbirth — this is directly stated in NCERT. Vasopressin (Option A) acts on the kidney to reabsorb water, not on the uterus. Prolactin (Option B) regulates mammary gland growth and milk formation, but does not cause uterine contraction. Melatonin (Option D) regulates the sleep-wake cycle and body rhythms, and has no role in uterine contraction.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: In females, it stimulates a vigorous contraction of uterus at the time of child birth, and milk ejection from the mammary gland.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Deficiency of iodine in the diet leads to hypothyroidism. Which condition can result from hypothyroidism during pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Hypothyroidism during pregnancy leads to cretinism in the growing baby, which includes stunted growth, mental retardation, and deaf-mutism — clearly stated in NCERT. Acromegaly (Option A) is caused by excess growth hormone secretion in adults, not by iodine deficiency. Diabetes Insipidus (Option C) results from impaired ADH synthesis or release, leading to water loss, and is unrelated to thyroid hormone deficiency. Graves' disease (Option D) is actually a form of hyperthyroidism, which is the opposite condition of hypothyroidism.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: Hypothyroidism during pregnancy causes defective development and maturation of the growing baby leading to stunted growth (cretinism), mental retardation, low intelligence quotient, abnormal skin, deaf-mutism, etc.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In monocotyledonous plants, what happens to the primary root?

options.A: It grows deep into the soil and forms a strong tap root
options.B: It is short lived and is replaced by a large number of roots
options.C: It produces secondary and tertiary lateral roots
options.D: It elongates directly from the radicle and persists throughout

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that in monocots the primary root is short lived and is replaced by many roots originating from the base of the stem (fibrous root system). Option A describes a tap root system which is seen in dicots, not monocots. Option C describes lateral root formation which is a feature of the tap root system in dicots. Option D describes the persistence of the primary root from the radicle, which is again a dicot feature.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In monocotyledonous plants, the primary root is short lived and is replaced by a large number of roots.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Roots that arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle are called:
questions.explanation: NCERT defines adventious roots as roots that arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle. Option A (tap roots) arise from direct elongation of the radicle in dicots. Option B (fibrous roots) originate from the base of the stem in monocots and replace the primary root. Option D (lateral roots) are branches of the primary root, also originating from the radicle region.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In some plants, like grass, Monstera and the banyan tree, roots arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle and are called adventious roots.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The primary root and its branches together form which type of root system?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that the primary root and its branches (secondary, tertiary roots) together form the tap root system, as seen in mustard. Option A (fibrous root system) is formed in monocots where the primary root is replaced by many roots arising from the stem base. Option B (adventious root system) refers to roots arising from parts other than the radicle. Option D (prop root system) is a modification of adventious roots, not a primary root system type.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The primary roots and its branches constitute the tap root system, as seen in the mustard plant.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a pinnately compound leaf, the leaflets are present on a common axis called the rachis. Which plant is given as an example of a pinnately compound leaf in NCERT?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states neem as the example of a pinnately compound leaf. Silk cotton is wrong because it is the example of a palmately compound leaf, where leaflets are attached at the tip of the petiole. China rose and Guava are examples used for phyllotaxy, not pinnately compound leaves.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In a pinnately compound leaf a number of leaflets are present on a common axis, the rachis, which represents the midrib of the leaf as in neem.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Root hairs arise from the epidermal cells of a specific region of the root. Which region of the root do root hairs originate from?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that root hairs are formed from epidermal cells of the region of maturation (also called the zone of maturation or root hair zone), which is proximal to the region of elongation. In this region, some epidermal cells elongate and form fine, delicate, thread-like structures called root hairs that absorb water and minerals from the soil. Region of elongation is incorrect because cells there are undergoing rapid elongation. Region of meristematic activity is incorrect because those cells are small, thin-walled and actively dividing. Root cap is incorrect because it is a thimble-like protective structure covering the root apex.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: From this region some of the epidermal cells form very fine and delicate, thread-like structures called root hairs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which cells present in the interstial spaces of the testis synthesise and secrete androgens?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, Leydig cells (interstial cells) present in the interstial spaces of the testis are responsible for synthesing and secreting androgens (testicular hormones). Sertoli cells provide nutrition to developing germ cells. Spermatogonia are male germ cells that undergo meiosis to ultimately form sperms. Spermatocytes are intermediate stages in sperm formation, not hormone-secreting cells. Note: This topic belongs to the chapter 'Human Reproduction' (Chapter 3 in Class 12 Biology), not 'Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: Leydig cells synthesise and secrete testicular hormones called androgens.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the correct sequence of male sex accessory ducts through which sperms travel from the testis toward the urethra?

options.A: Rete testis → Vasa efferentia → Epidymis → Vas deferens
options.B: Epidymis → Rete testis → Vasa efferentia → Vas deferens
options.C: Vasa efferentia → Rete testis → Vas deferens → Epidymis
options.D: Rete testis → Epidymis → Vasa efferentia → Vas deferens

questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 3 (Human Reproduction), the correct sequence is: seminiferous tubules → rete testis → vasa efferentia → epidymis → vas deferens → urethra. Option A correctly states: Rete testis → Vasa efferentia → Epidymis → Vas deferens. Option B incorrectly places epidymis before rete testis. Option C incorrectly puts vasa efferentia before rete testis. Option D wrongly places epidymis before vasa efferentia. Note: The NCERT line cited belongs to Chapter 3 'Human Reproduction' (Class 12), not 'Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants' which is Chapter 2.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The seminiferous tubules of the testis open into the vasa efferentia through rete testis. The vasa efferentia leave the testis and open into epidymis located along the posterior surface of each testis. The epidymis leads to vas deferens.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which component is the seminal plasma rich in, as secreted by the male accessory glands?
questions.explanation: NCERT specifically states that seminal plasma is rich in fructose, calcium and certain enzymes. Option A is wrong because glucose and sodium are not mentioned; seminal plasma contains fructose, not glucose. Option C is wrong because sucrose and potassium are not mentioned in the text. Option D is wrong because galactose and magnesium are not mentioned in the NCERT description of seminal plasma.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: Secretions of these glands constitute the seminal plasma which is rich in fructose, calcium and certain enzymes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The inner glandular layer of the uterus that lines the uterine cavity is called:
questions.explanation: The endometrium is the inner glandular layer that lines the uterine cavity — directly stated in NCERT Biology Chapter 'Human Reproduction' (Class 12, Chapter 3). Option A (myometrium) is wrong because myometrium is the middle thick layer of smooth muscle, not the inner glandular layer. Option B (perimetrium) is wrong because perimetrium is the external thin membranous layer. Option D (cervical canal) is wrong because that is the cavity of the cervix, not a layer of the uterine wall. Note: This topic belongs to NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 'Human Reproduction', not 'Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants' (which is Chapter 2 dealing with plants).
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: inner glandular layer called endometrium that lines the uterine cavity.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The process by which spermatids are transformed into spermatozoa is called:
questions.explanation: Spermiogenesis is the process that transforms spermatids into spermatozoa — this is directly stated in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 3 (Human Reproduction). Option A (spermatogenesis) is wrong because spermatogenesis refers to the entire process of sperm production from spermatogonia, not just the final transformation step. Option B (spermiation) is wrong because spermiation is the release of sperms from seminiferous tubules, not the transformation of spermatids. Option C (gametogenesis) is wrong because gametogenesis is the broad term for gamete production in both sexes, not specifically the transformation of spermatids. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 'Human Reproduction' (Class 12), not 'Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The spermatids are transformed into spermatozoa (sperms) by the process called spermiogenesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In sponges, the cells are arranged as loose cell aggregates. What level of organisation does this represent?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that sponges show cellular level of organisation because their cells exist as loose aggregates. Option A (tissue level) is seen in coelenterates, where cells performing the same function group together. Option B (organ level) is seen in Platyhelminthes and higher phyla. Option D (organ system level) is seen in Annelids, Arthropods, Molluscs, Echinoderms and Chordates.
questions.ncert_line: in sponges, the cells are arranged as loose cell aggregates, i.e., they exhibit cellular level of organisation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In an open type circulatory system, what happens to the blood after it is pumped out of the heart?

options.A: It flows through arteries, veins and capillaries only
options.B: It is circulated through vessels of varying diameters
options.C: It directly bathes the cells and tissues
options.D: It enters the coelom and mixes with coelomic fluid

questions.explanation: NCERT states that in the open type circulatory system, blood is pumped out of the heart and directly bathes the cells and tissues. Option A and B describe the closed type circulatory system, where blood circulates through vessels like arteries, veins and capillaries. Option D mentions coelomic fluid mixing, which is not described in NCERT for the open circulatory system.
questions.ncert_line: open type in which the blood is pumped out of the heart and the cells and tissues are directly bathed in it.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following groups of animals shows bilateral symmetry?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that annelids and arthropods exhibit bilateral symmetry, where the body is divisible into identical left and right halves in only one plane. Option A is wrong because coelenterates show radial symmetry (ctenophores also show radial symmetry). Option B is wrong because sponges are mostly asymmetrical and echinoderms show radial symmetry as adults. Option D is wrong because both ctenophores and echinoderms show radial symmetry, not bilateral symmetry.
questions.ncert_line: Animals like annelids, arthropods, etc., where the body can be divided into identical left and right halves in only one plane, exhibit bilateral symmetry.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In Phylum Porifera, water enters the body through minute pores called ostia and exits through which structure?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, water exits the sponge body through the osculum. Option B (spongocoel) is wrong because spongocoel is the central cavity where water collects, not where it exits. Option C (choanocytes) is wrong because choanocytes are collar cells that line the spongocoel and canals — they are not an exit point. Option D (spicules) is wrong because spicules are part of the skeleton that supports the body, not part of the water transport pathway.
questions.ncert_line: Water enters through minute pores (ostia) in the body wall into a central cavity, spongocoel, from where it goes out through the osculum.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which phenomenon is well-marked in Ctenophores, where a living organism has the ability to emit light?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that bioluminescence — the property of a living organism to emit light — is well-marked in ctenophores. Option B (metagenesis) is wrong because metagenesis refers to alternation of generations seen in cnidarians like Obelia, not ctenophores. Option C (phosphorescence) is wrong because phosphorescence is a physical phenomenon of non-living materials absorbing and re-emitting light, not a biological property. Option D (chemiluminescence) is wrong because chemiluminescence refers to light produced by a chemical reaction in non-living systems, whereas NCERT specifically uses the term bioluminescence for living organisms.
questions.ncert_line: Bioluminescence (the property of a living organism to emit light) is well-marked in ctenophores.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of classification system for flowering plants was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker?
questions.explanation: The natural classification system, which is based on natural affinities and considers external as well as internal features, was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker — as directly stated in NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter 3 (Plant Kingdom). Artificial classification systems were based on only a few gross morphological characters and were not given by Bentham and Hooker. Phylogenetic classification systems are based on evolutionary relationships and are accepted at present, but are not attributed to Bentham and Hooker. Numerical taxonomy uses computers and codes for all observable characters, which is a separate modern approach.
questions.ncert_line: Such a classification for flowering plants was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following organisms shows oogamous sexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Volvox shows oogamous reproduction, where a large non-motile female gamete fuses with a smaller motile male gamete — this is directly stated in NCERT. Spirogyra shows isogamous reproduction involving non-flagellated but similar-sized gametes, so it is wrong. Ulothrix shows isogamous reproduction with flagellated, similar-sized gametes, so it is incorrect. Eudorina shows anisogamous reproduction where gametes differ in size but are both motile, not oogamous, so it is also wrong.
questions.ncert_line: Fusion between one large, non-motile (static) female gamete and a smaller, motile male gamete is termed oogamous, e.g., Volvox, Fucus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which pigment gives red algae their characteristic red colour?
questions.explanation: r-Phycoerythrin is the red pigment that dominates in Rhodophyceae, giving them their red colour — stated directly in NCERT. Option A (fucoxanthin) is a xanthophyll pigment found in brown algae (Phaeophyceae), not red algae. Option C (chlorophyll b) is the pigment associated with green algae (Chlorophyceae). Option D (carotenoid) is present in various algae but is not the pigment responsible for the characteristic red colour of Rhodophyceae.
questions.ncert_line: The members of rhodophyceae are commonly called red algae because of the predominance of the red pigment, r-phycoerythrin in their body.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which organelle are pyrenoids located in green algae?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that pyrenoids are located inside the chloroplasts of green algae, where they store protein and starch. Option A (nucleus) controls genetic activity and does not contain pyrenoids. Option B (mitochondria) is the site of cellular respiration and has no connection to pyrenoids. Option D (vacuole) is a storage compartment for cell sap and is not the location of pyrenoids.
questions.ncert_line: Most of the members have one or more storage bodies called pyrenoids located in the chloroplasts.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The number of species that are known and described on Earth ranges between which values?
questions.explanation: NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter 1 (The Living World) directly states that the number of species known and described on Earth ranges between 1.7–1.8 million. Option B (1.2–1.5 million) is too low and not mentioned in the text. Option C (2.0–2.5 million) exceeds the stated range. Option D (0.5–1.0 million) is far below the value given in NCERT.
questions.ncert_line: The number of species that are known and described range between 1.7-1.8 million.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ernst Mayr pioneered which important concept in biology?

options.A: The currently accepted definition of a biological species
options.B: The binomial nomenclature system for naming organisms
options.C: The five-kingdom classification of living organisms
options.D: The theory of inheritance of acquired characteristics

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Ernst Mayr pioneered the currently accepted definition of a biological species. Option B (binomial nomenclature) was developed by Linnaeus, not Mayr. Option C (five-kingdom classification) is associated with Whittaker. Option D (inheritance of acquired characteristics) is associated with Lamarck.
questions.ncert_line: He also pioneered the currently accepted definition of a biological species.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What term refers to the number and types of organisms present on Earth?
questions.explanation: NCERT defines biodiversity as the number and types of organisms present on Earth. Option B (Taxonomy) refers to the science of identification, nomenclature, and classification of organisms. Option C (Systematics) deals with the diversity of organisms and their evolutionary relationships. Option D (Nomenclature) refers to the system of naming organisms, not counting their diversity.
questions.ncert_line: This refers to biodiversity or the number and types of organisms present on earth.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The scientific name of mango is Mangifera indica. In this name, what does 'indica' represent?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, 'indica' in Mangifera indica is the specific epithet — the second component of a binomial name. Option A is wrong because Mangifera is the generic name, not indica. Option C is wrong because the author's abbreviation (e.g., Linn.) appears after the specific epithet, separately. Option D is wrong because family is a higher taxonomic category and is not part of the binomial name.
questions.ncert_line: In this name Mangifera represents the genus while indica, is a particular species, or a specific epithet.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which system of providing scientific names to organisms uses two components — the generic name and the specific epithet?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that the system with two components — generic name and specific epithet — is called Binomial nomenclature, given by Carolus Linnaeus. Option A is wrong because trinomial nomenclature uses three components, which is not described here. Option C is wrong because ICBN is the code that provides rules for naming plants, not the name of the two-component system itself. Option D is wrong because taxonomic classification is the process of grouping organisms, not the naming system.
questions.ncert_line: Each name has two components – the Generic name and the specific epithet. This system of providing a name with two components is called Binomial nomenclature.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which membrane covers the eyes of a frog and protects them while in water?
questions.explanation: The nictitating membrane covers and protects the frog's eyes in water — directly stated in NCERT. Option A is wrong because the tympanic membrane is the ear structure that receives sound signals, not an eye covering. Option C is wrong because mucous membrane keeps the skin moist, it is not related to the eyes. Option D is wrong because the peritoneal membrane lines the body cavity and has no role in eye protection.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: Eyes are bulged and covered by a nictitating membrane that protects them while in water.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the protective coloration seen in frogs, where they change colour to hide from their enemies, called?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that the protective coloration used by frogs to hide from enemies is called micry. Option A is wrong because aestivation refers to summer sleep in frogs, not colour change. Option B is wrong because hibernation refers to winter sleep in frogs. Option D is wrong because poikilothermy refers to the inability to maintain a constant body temperature, not colour change.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: They have the ability to change the colour to hide them from their enemies (camouflage). This protective coloration is called micry.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which feature is present in male frogs but absent in female frogs?

options.B: A nictitating membrane over the eyes
options.C: Vocal sacs and a copulatory pad on the first digit of fore limbs
options.D: A tympanum on either side of the eyes

questions.explanation: NCERT states that vocal sacs and the copulatory pad on the first digit of fore limbs are present only in male frogs, making these features of sexual dimorphism. Option A is wrong because webbed digits are present in both male and female frogs and help in swimming. Option B is wrong because the nictitating membrane is present in all frogs to protect the eyes in water. Option D is wrong because the tympanum is a general feature of frogs used to receive sound signals and is not exclusive to males.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: Male frogs can be distinguished by the presence of sound producing vocal sacs and also a copulatory pad on the first digit of the fore limbs which are absent in female frogs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a frog, bile from the gall bladder and pancreatic juice from the pancreas both reach the duodenum through which structure?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that both bile and pancreatic juice enter the duodenum through a common bile duct. Option B (hepatic portal vein) is a blood vessel that connects the intestine and liver — it does not carry digestive secretions. Option C (cystic duct) is a real anatomical term related to the gall bladder but is not mentioned in NCERT as the route to the duodenum. Option D (renal portal vessel) connects the kidney and lower body parts and has nothing to do with digestion.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: The duodenum receives bile from gall bladder and pancreatic juices from the pancreas through a common bile duct.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During aestivation and hibernation in frogs, gaseous exchange takes place through which organ?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text explicitly states that during aestivation and hibernation, gaseous exchange occurs through the skin. Frogs are amphibians that can perform cutaneous respiration (breathing through moist skin), which is sufficient for their reduced metabolic needs during dormancy periods like aestivation (summer dormancy) and hibernation (winter dormancy). Option B (lungs) is used during pulmonary respiration on land but not specifically during aestivation or hibernation. Option C (buccal cavity) is one of the respiratory organs on land but is not mentioned for aestivation or hibernation. Option D (nostrils) are simply the entry point for air and are not a site of gaseous exchange.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: During aestivation and hibernation gaseous exchange takes place through skin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During which phase of the cell cycle does DNA replication take place in eukaryotic cells?
questions.explanation: S phase (Synthesis phase) is specifically the stage when DNA replication occurs — this is directly stated in NCERT. G1 phase is wrong because during G1 the cell grows but does not replicate its DNA. G2 phase is wrong because during G2, proteins are synthesised in preparation for mitosis, not DNA. M phase is wrong because M phase is when actual cell division occurs, not DNA replication.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: S or synthesis phase marks the period during which DNA synthesis or replication takes place.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What happens to cells that exit the G1 phase and enter the quiescent stage (G0)?

options.B: They prepare for mitosis by synthesing proteins
options.D: They immediately enter the S phase

questions.explanation: Cells in G0 are in an inactive, quiescent stage and do not divide further — this is clearly stated in NCERT. Option A is wrong because G0 cells are inactive and do not replicate DNA. Option B is wrong because protein synthesis for mitosis happens in G2 phase, not in G0. Option D is wrong because G0 cells have exited the active cell cycle and do not proceed to S phase.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: These cells that do not divide further exit G1 phase to enter an inactive stage called quiescent stage (G0) of the cell cycle.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a human cell with 2n chromosomes at the start of the S phase, what is the chromosome number at the end of the S phase?
questions.explanation: Although the amount of DNA doubles during S phase (from 2C to 4C content), the chromosome number does not change and stays at 2n — this is directly stated in NCERT. Each chromosome now consists of two sister chromatids joined at the centromere, but they are still counted as one chromosome. Option A (4n) is wrong because 4n refers to chromosome number doubling, which does not happen; only the DNA content doubles. Option B (n) is wrong because n represents haploid number, which occurs only after meiosis. Option D (3n) is wrong because 3n is a triploid condition, which does not occur during a normal S phase.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: There is no increase in the chromosome number; if the cell had diploid or 2n number of chromosomes at G1, even after S phase the number of chromosomes remains the same, i.e., 2n.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: At which stage of mitosis is the morphology of chromosomes most easily studied?

options.A: Prophase, because chromosomes begin to condense
options.B: Metaphase, because condensation is complete and chromosomes are clearly visible
options.C: Anaphase, because chromatids separate and move to poles
options.D: Telophase, because chromosomes reach the opposite poles

questions.explanation: During Metaphase, chromosome condensation is fully complete, making chromosomes clearly visible and easy to study under the microscope — this is directly stated in the NCERT text. Option A is wrong because during Prophase condensation only begins and is not yet complete. Option C is wrong because during Anaphase chromatids are separating and moving, making individual chromosome morphology harder to study. Option D is wrong because Telophase is the stage when chromosomes reach the poles and begin to decondense, not the best stage for morphology study.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: By this stage, condensation of chromosomes is completed and they can be observed clearly under the microscope. This then, is the stage at which morphology of chromosomes is most easily studied.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During anaphase of mitosis, which part of the chromosome remains directed towards the pole as the chromosome moves away from the equatorial plate?
questions.explanation: According to the NCERT text, during anaphase the centromere leads the movement towards the pole, remaing at the leading edge. Option A is wrong because the arms trail behind the centromere, they do not lead. Option B is wrong because the kinetochore is the site of spindle fibre attachment on the centromere, but the text specifically names the centromere as the leading part. Option D is wrong because sister chromatids have already separated at the start of anaphase and are now referred to as daughter chromosomes, not a structural leading part.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: As each chromosome moves away from the equatorial plate, the centromere of each chromosome remains directed towards the pole and hence at the leading edge, with the arms of the chromosome trailing behind.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Animals that excrete nitrogenous waste mainly as urea are called:
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that animals excreting urea are called ureotelic animals. Option B (ammonotelic) refers to animals that excrete ammonia, such as bony fishes and aquatic amphibians. Option C (uricotelic) refers to animals that excrete uric acid, such as reptiles and birds. Option D (osmoregulators) refers to organisms that regulate internal osmotic concentration, which is not the term for urea-excreting animals.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: Mammals, many terrestrial amphibians and marine fishes mainly excrete urea and are called ureotelic animals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which excretory structure is found in most insects including cockroaches?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Malpighian tubules are the excretory structures of most insects including cockroaches. Option B (Nephridia) are tubular excretory structures found in earthworms and other annelids. Option C (Protonephridia or flame cells) are found in Platyhelminthes, rotifers, some annelids, and Amphioxus. Option D (Antennal glands or green glands) perform excretory functions in crustaceans like prawns.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: Malpighian tubules are the excretory structures of most of the insects including cockroaches.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The notch present towards the centre of the inner concave surface of the kidney is called:
questions.explanation: NCERT states that the hilum is the notch on the inner concave surface of the kidney through which the ureter, blood vessels, and nerves enter. Option B (renal pelvis) is the broad funnel-shaped space found inner to the hilum, not the notch itself. Option C (calyx) refers to the projections of the renal pelvis. Option D (Columns of Bertini) are cortical extensions found between the medullary pyramids, not a surface notch.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: Towards the centre of the inner concave surface of the kidney is a notch called hilum through which ureter, blood vessels and nerves enter.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which cells of the Bowman's capsule are arranged in an intricate manner to leave minute spaces called filtration slits or slit pores?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that podocytes are the epithelial cells of Bowman's capsule that form filtration slits. Option B (mesangial cells) are found in the glomerulus but are not described as forming filtration slits. Option C (juxtaglomerular cells) are located at the contact point of the afferent arteriole and DCT, involved in GFR regulation, not filtration slits. Option D (cuboidal epithelial cells) describes the ling of PCT, not Bowman's capsule.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: The epithelial cells of Bowman's capsule called podocytes are arranged in an intricate manner so as to leave some minute spaces called filtration slits or slit pores.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What type of epithelium lines the Proximal Convoluted Tubule (PCT), which increases its surface area for reabsorption?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that PCT is lined by simple cuboidal brush border epithelium, and the brush border (microvilli) increases surface area for reabsorption. Option B (simple squamous epithelium) is thin and flat, found in structures like Bowman's capsule, not PCT. Option C (stratified columnar epithelium) is multilayered and not found in nephron tubules. Option D (ciliated cuboidal epithelium) introduces cilia, which are not mentioned for PCT in NCERT.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: PCT is lined by simple cuboidal brush border epithelium which increases the surface area for reabsorption.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who disproved the 'good humor' hypothesis of health by demonstrating normal body temperature in persons with blackbile?
questions.explanation: William Harvey used the experimental method and a thermometer to show that people with blackbile had normal body temperature, which disproved the 'good humor' hypothesis. Hippocrates was one of the early Greeks who actually supported the humor hypothesis, not disproved it. Norman Borlaug was associated with the Green Revolution in wheat. M.S. Swaminathan was an Indian plant geneticist — neither of these two were involved in this medical discovery. This is directly stated in NCERT Biology textbook in the chapter on Evolution.
questions.ncert_line: The discovery of blood circulation by William Harvey using experimental method and the demonstration of normal body temperature in persons with blackbile using thermometer disproved the 'good humor' hypothesis of health.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following best defines health according to the NCERT text?

options.A: A state of complete physical, mental and social well-being
options.B: Absence of disease and physical fitness
options.C: A balance of humors in the body and mind
options.D: The ability to fight infectious diseases effectively

questions.explanation: The NCERT text explicitly defines health as a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being. Option B is directly rejected by the text, which states health does not simply mean absence of disease or physical fitness. Option C describes the old Greek/Ayurveda view that was later disproved. Option D is not the definition given in the text — fighting infections is one aspect but not the complete definition. Note: This content appears in the NCERT Biology chapter on Human Health and Disease, not Evolution.
questions.ncert_line: Health does not simply mean 'absence of disease' or 'physical fitness'. It could be defined as a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Among non-infectious diseases, which is stated as the major cause of death in the NCERT text?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that among non-infectious diseases, cancer is the major cause of death. AIDS is an infectious disease caused by HIV, not a non-infectious one. Drug abuse adversely affects health but is not stated as the major cause of death among non-infectious diseases. Genetic disorders are a cause of disease but are not specifically identified as the major cause of death in this context. Note: This topic is covered in the NCERT Biology chapter on Human Health and Disease, not Evolution.
questions.ncert_line: Among non-infectious diseases, cancer is the major cause of death.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which toxic substance is released when red blood cells rupture during malaria infection?
questions.explanation: Haemozoin is the toxic substance released during RBC rupture in malaria and it causes recurring chills and high fever — stated directly in NCERT. Option B (Haemoglobin) is a normal protein in RBCs, not a toxin released during rupture. Option C (Sporozoite) is the infectious form of Plasmodium that enters the body, not a toxic substance released from RBCs. Option D (Histamine) is a chemical involved in allergic reactions, not associated with malarial RBC rupture.
questions.ncert_line: The rupture of RBCs is associated with release of a toxic substance, haemozoin, which is responsible for the chill and high fever recurring every three to four days.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which bacterium is responsible for causing typhoid fever in human beings?
questions.explanation: Salmonella typhi is directly named in NCERT as the bacterium that causes typhoid fever. Option B (Streptoccus pneumoniae) is a bacterium that causes pneumonia, not typhoid. Option C (Haemophilus influenzae) also causes pneumonia by infecting the alveoli of the lungs. Option D (Entamoeba histolytica) is a protozoan parasite that causes amoebiasis (amoebic dysentery), not typhoid fever. Note: This topic is covered in the Human Health and Disease chapter, not Evolution.
questions.ncert_line: Salmonella typhi is a pathogenic bacterium which causes typhoid fever in human beings.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following organisms uses moist cuticle for exchange of gases?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that earthworms use their moist cuticle for gas exchange. Insects are wrong because they use tracheal tubes, not cuticle. Sponges are wrong because they use simple diffusion over their entire body surface. Fish are wrong because they use gills for gas exchange.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: Earthworms use their moist cuticle and insects have a network of tubes (tracheal tubes) to transport atmospheric air within the body.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the process of exchange of oxygen from the atmosphere with carbon dioxide produced by the cells called?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly defines breathing as the process of exchange of O2 from the atmosphere with CO2 produced by cells. Digestion is wrong because it refers to breakdown of food. Circulation is wrong because it refers to transport of blood through the body. Excretion is wrong because it refers to removal of metabolic wastes from the body.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: This process of exchange of O2 from the atmosphere with CO2 produced by the cells is called breathing, commonly known as respiration.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of respiration is used by amphibians like frogs through their skin?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that amphibians like frogs can respire through their moist skin, which is called cutaneous respiration. Pulmonary respiration is wrong because that refers to respiration through lungs used by terrestrial forms. Branchial respiration is wrong because that refers to respiration through gills used by aquatic arthropods and molluscs. Tracheal respiration is wrong because that refers to the system used by insects involving tracheal tubes.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: Amphibians like frogs can respire through their moist skin (cutaneous respiration) also.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which structure covers the glottis during swallowing to prevent food from entering the larynx?
questions.explanation: The epiglottis is a thin elastic cartilaginous flap that covers the glottis during swallowing — this is directly stated in NCERT. Option B (uvula) is a fleshy extension of the soft palate that helps in closing the nasal passage during swallowing, not the larynx. Option C (soft palate) prevents food from entering the nasal cavity, not the larynx. Option D (vocal cord) is involved in sound production inside the larynx and does not cover the glottis during swallowing.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: During swallowing glottis can be covered by a thin elastic cartilaginous flap called epiglottis to prevent the entry of food into the larynx.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: At which level does the trachea divide into the right and left primary bronchi?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 11, Chapter 17 - Breathing and Exchange of Gases), the trachea divides at the level of the 5th thoracic vertebra into right and left primary bronchi. Option B (3rd thoracic vertebra) is a higher level and is not mentioned in NCERT. Option C (7th cervical vertebra) is in the neck region, which is incorrect. Option D (1st lumbar vertebra) is below the thoracic region and is anatomically impossible for tracheal bifurcation.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: Trachea is a straight tube extending up to the mid-thoracic cavity, which divides at the level of 5th thoracic vertebra into a right and left primary bronchi.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which scientist proposed the Five Kingdom Classification in 1969?
questions.explanation: R.H. Whittaker proposed the Five Kingdom Classification in 1969 — this is directly stated in NCERT. Aristotle attempted early scientific classification but only divided plants into trees, shrubs and herbs. Linnaeus developed the Two Kingdom system with Plantae and Animalia. Charles Darwin is not mentioned in this context in the text.
questions.ncert_line: R.H. Whittaker (1969) proposed a Five Kingdom Classification. The kingdoms defined by him were named Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What type of cell wall is present in the cell of fungi?

options.C: Noncellulosic wall made of polysaccharide and amino acid

questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that fungi have chitin in their cell walls. Cellulose is the cell wall component of Plantae, not fungi. Noncellulosic wall made of polysaccharide and amino acid describes the cell wall of Monera (bacteria). Animalia is the kingdom that lacks a cell wall entirely.
questions.ncert_line: The classification did not differentiate between the heterotrophic group – fungi, and the autotrophic green plants, though they also showed a characteristic difference in their walls composition – the fungi had chitin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What was the main character that unified the plant kingdom in earlier classification systems?

options.C: All organisms had a cell wall in their cells

questions.explanation: According to NCERT, the presence of a cell wall was the unifying character that grouped all organisms under 'Plants' in earlier systems. All organisms being photosynthetic is wrong because fungi (included under plants earlier) are heterotrophic, not photosynthetic. All organisms being eukaryotic is wrong because bacteria and blue-green algae, which are prokaryotic, were also included under plants in earlier classification. Sexual reproduction is not mentioned as the unifying character in the text.
questions.ncert_line: Earlier classification systems included bacteria, blue green algae, fungi, mosses, ferns, gymnosperms and the angiosperms under 'Plants'. The character that unified this whole kingdom was that all the organisms included had a cell wall in their cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which specialised cells in cyanobacteria are responsible for fixing atmospheric nitrogen?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that heterocysts are the specialised cells in cyanobacteria (like Nostoc and Anabaena) that fix atmospheric nitrogen. Option B (mesomes) are membrane infoldings in bacteria involved in respiration, not nitrogen fixation. Option C (chromatophores) are pigment-bearing structures found in some bacteria, unrelated to nitrogen fixation. Option D (akinetes) are thick-walled resting spores found in some cyanobacteria but are not involved in nitrogen fixation.
questions.ncert_line: Some of these organisms can fix atmospheric nitrogen in specialised cells called heterocysts, e.g., Nostoc and Anabaena.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which group of archaebacteria is responsible for the production of methane from the dung of ruminant animals?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, methanogens live in the gut of ruminants like cows and buffaloes and produce methane (biogas) from their dung. Option B (halophiles) are archaebacteria that live in extremely salty areas, not in ruminant guts. Option C (thermoacidophiles) are archaebacteria that survive in hot springs and acidic environments, not in the gut of animals. Option D (cyanobacteria) are photosynthetic eubacteria and are not archaebacteria at all.
questions.ncert_line: Methanogens are present in the gut of several ruminant animals such as cows and buffaloes and they are responsible for the production of methane (biogas) from the dung of these animals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which protein in plasma is mainly responsible for osmotic balance of blood?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that albumins help in osmotic balance. Fibrinogen is wrong because it is need for clotting of blood, not osmotic balance. Globulin is wrong because it is primarily involved in defense mechanisms of the body. Haemoglobin is wrong because it is found in RBCs, not plasma, and it transports respiratory gases.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: the albumins help in osmotic balance.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of white blood cells are responsible for releasing histamine, serotonin, and heparin during inflammatory reactions?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that basophils secrete histamine, serotonin, and heparin and are involved in inflammatory reactions. Neutrophils are wrong because they are phagocytic cells that destroy foreign organisms. Eosinophils are wrong because they resist infections and are associated with allergic reactions. Lymphocytes are wrong because they are responsible for immune responses of the body.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: Basophils secrete histamine, serotonin, heparin, etc., and are involved in inflammatory reactions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where are red blood cells (RBCs) formed in adult humans?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that RBCs are formed in the red bone marrow in adults. Spleen is wrong because it is actually the site where old RBCs are destroyed — it is called the graveyard of RBCs, not the site of formation. Liver is wrong because it is not mentioned as the site of RBC formation in adults (though it does function as a site of RBC production in the fetus). Lymph nodes are wrong because they are associated with the lymphatic system and production of certain white blood cells, not RBCs.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: RBCs are formed in the red bone marrow in the adults.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A pregnant woman is Rh negative and her foetus is Rh positive. During the delivery of her first child, the mother's blood gets exposed to the Rh positive blood of the foetus. What happens as a result of this exposure?

options.A: The mother starts preparing antibodies against Rh antigen in her blood
options.B: The foetus immediately develops erythroblastosis foetalis during the first pregnancy
options.C: The placenta breaks down and mixes both blood types permanently
options.D: The mother's RBCs are destroyed by the Rh antigens of the foetus
