questions.explanation: Carboxylation is the most crucial step of the Calvin cycle where CO₂ is utilised for the carboxylation of RuBP. This reaction is catalysed by enzyme RuBP carboxylase. Since this enzyme also has an oxygenase activity, RuBisCO has higher affinity for carbon dioxide than oxygen. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 6, Q114]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The protein portion of an enzyme is called:
questions.explanation: There are number of cases in which non-protein constituents called co-factors are bound to the enzyme to make the enzyme catalytically active. In these instances, the protein portion of the enzymes is called the apoenzyme. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 7, Q116]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following enzyme(s) are NOT essential for gene cloning?
questions.explanation: a. B and C only b. C and D only c. A and B only d. D and E only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following type of immunity is present at the time of birth and is a non-specific type of defence in the human body?
questions.explanation: Innate immunity is non-specific type of defence, that is present at the time of birth. This is accomplished by providing different types of barriers to the entry of the foreign agents into our body. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 7-8, Q118]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which factor is important for termination of transcription?
questions.explanation: In prokaryotes the RNA polymerase is only capable of catalysing the process of elongation. It associates transiently with initiation factor (σ) and termination factor (ρ) to initiate and terminate the transcription respectively. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 8, Q119]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following hormones released from the pituitary is actually synthesized in the hypothalamus?
questions.explanation: Neurohypophysis i.e., posterior pituitary stores and releases two hormones called oxytocin and vasopressin (Also called ADH i.e., antidiuretic hormone) which are actually synthesised by hypothalamus and are transported axonally to neurohypophysis. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 8, Q120]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following microbes is NOT involved in the preparation of household products?
questions.explanation: a. C and E only b. A and B only c. A and C only d. C and D only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Role of the water vascular system in Echinoderms is:

options.D: Digestion and Respiration E. Digestion and Excretion

questions.explanation: a. B, D and E Only b. A and B Only c. A and C Only d. B and C Only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Read the following statements on plant growth and development:

options.A:
Parthenocarpy can be induced by auxins.
(B) Plant growth regulators can be involved in promotion as well as inhibition of growth.
(C) Dedifferentiation is a pre-requisite for re-differentiation.
(D) Abscisic acid is a plant growth promoter.
(E) Apical dominance promotes the growth of lateral buds.

questions.explanation: a. B, D, E only b. A, B, C only c. A, C, E only d. A, D, E only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Why can't insulin be given orally to diabetic patients?

options.B: Human body will elicit strong immune response
options.C: It will be digested in Gastro-Intestinal (GI) tract

questions.explanation: Insulin can't be administered orally to diabetic patients as being the proteinaceous molecule, it will be digested in gastro-intestinal tract. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 11, Q128]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following is the characteristic feature of gymnosperms?
questions.explanation: The gymnosperms (Gymnos: naked, sperma: seed) are plants in which the ovules are not enclosed by an ovary wall and remains exposed, both before and after fertilization. The seeds that develop post-fertilization, are not covered, i.e., naked. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 11, Q129]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Silencing of specific mRNA is possible via RNAi because of:
questions.explanation: RNAi (RNA interference) takes place in all eukaryotic organisms as a method of cellular defense. This method involves silencing of a specific mRNA due to a complementary dsRNA molecule that binds to and prevents translation of the mRNA. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 11, Q131]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Twins are born to a family that lives next door to you. The twins are a boy and a girl. Which of the following must be true?

options.D: They were conceived through in vitro fertilization

questions.explanation: Fraternal twins or dizygotic twins are 2 separate fertilized eggs, they usually develop 2 separate amniotic sacs, placentas and supporting structures. If twins are a boy and a girl, this indicates they are fraternal twins. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 12, Q132]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cardiac activities of the heart are regulated by:

options.B: A special neural centre in the medulla oblongata

questions.explanation: a. A, B and D Only b. A, B and C Only c. A, B, C and D d. A, C and D Only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Streptokinase produced by bacterium Streptococcus is used for:
questions.explanation: Streptokinase produced by bacterium Streptococcus is used for removing clots from blood vessels. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 13, Q137]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

options.A: : A typical unfertilised, angiosperm embryo sac at maturity is 8 nucleate and 7-celled.

questions.explanation: In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer:

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Neoplastic characteristics of cells refer to:

options.C: Invasion and damage to the surrounding tissue

questions.explanation: a. B, C, D only b. A, B only c. A, B, C only d. A, B, D only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Given below are the stages in the life cycle of pteridophytes. Arrange the following stages in the correct sequence.

options.D: Formation of archegonia and antheridia in gametophyte E. Transfer of antherozoids to the archegonia in presence of water

questions.explanation: a. E, D, C, B, A b. B, A, D, E, C c. B, A, E, C, D d. D, E, C, A, B

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following enzymes contains 'Haem' as the prosthetic group?
questions.explanation: In peroxidase and catalase, which catalyze the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide to water and oxygen, haem is the prosthetic group and it is part of the active site of the enzymes. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 16, Q143]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Find the statement that is NOT correct with regard to the structure of monocot stem:

options.D: Vascular bundles are conjoint and closed

questions.explanation: In monocot stem, hypodermis is sclerenchymatous. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 16, Q145]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statement is correct about location of the male frog copulatory pad?

options.A: First digit of the fore limb
options.B: First and Second digit of fore limb

questions.explanation: In male frogs, copulatory pad is present on the first digit of the forelimbs which are absent in female frogs. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 17, Q146]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplifies DNA following the equation:
questions.explanation: PCR i.e., polymerase chain reaction amplifies DNA as per the equation 2ⁿ, where 'n' refers to number of cycles. Thus, if 3 PCR cycles will run, then 2³ i.e., 8 DNA fragments will be formed. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 17, Q149]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: From the statements given below choose the correct option:

options.A: The eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S and prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S.
options.C: The two sub-units of 80S ribosome are 60S and 40S while that of 70S are 50S and 30S.
options.D: The two sub-units of 80S ribosome are 60S and 20S and that of 70S are 50S and 20S. E. The two sub-units of 80S are 60S and 30S and that of 70S are 50S and 30S.

questions.explanation: Answer: b

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Each of the following characteristics represent a Kingdom proposed by Whittaker. Arrange the following in increasing order of complexity of body organization.

options.A: Multicellular heterotrophs with cell wall made of chitin.
options.B: Heterotrophs with tissue/organ/organ system level of body organization.
options.C: Prokaryotes with cell wall made of polysaccharides and amino acids.
options.D: Eukaryotic autotrophs with tissue/organ level of body organization. E. Eukaryotes with cellular body organization.

questions.explanation: a. C, E, A, B, D b. A, C, E, B, D c. C, E, A, D, B d. A, C, E, D, B

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The correct sequence of events in the life cycle of bryophytes is:

options.C: Reduction division to produce haploid spores.
options.D: Formation of sporophyte. E. Release of antherozoids into water.

questions.explanation: a. D, E, A, B, C b. D, E, A, C, B c. B, E, A, C, D d. B, E, A, D, C

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which are correct:

options.A: Computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging detect cancers of internal organs.
options.B: Chemotherapeutic drugs are used to kill non-cancerous cells.
options.C: α-interferon activate the cancer patients' immune system and helps in destroying the tumour.
options.D: Chemotherapeutic drugs are biological response modifiers. E. In the case of leukaemia blood cell counts are decreased.

questions.explanation: a. A and C only b. B and D only c. D and E only d. C and D only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Find the correct statement:

options.A:
In human pregnancy, the major organ systems are formed at the end of 12 weeks.
(B) In human pregnancy the major organ systems are formed at the end of 8 weeks.
(C) In human pregnancy heart is formed after one month of gestation.
(D) In human pregnancy, limbs and digits develop by the end of second month.
(E) In human pregnancy the appearance of hair is usually observed in the fifth month.

questions.explanation: a. A, C, D and E only b. A and E only c. B and C only d. B, C, D and E only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of non-distilled alcoholic beverage produced by yeast?
questions.explanation: Wine and beer are produced without distillation whereas whisky, brandy and rum are produced by distillation of fermented broth. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 21, Q158]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Given below are two statements:
questions.explanation: In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer:

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following diagrams is correct with regard to the proximal (P) and distal (D) tubule of the Nephron?

options.A: Both P and D with reabsorption and secretion arrows
options.B: P with secretion only, D with reabsorption only
options.C: P with reabsorption and secretion, D with reabsorption and secretion
options.D: P with reabsorption only, D with secretion only

questions.explanation: During urine formation, the tubular cells secrete substances like H⁺, K⁺ and ammonia into the filtrate. PCT → Selective secretion of H⁺, ammonia and K⁺ into the filtrate. DCT → Capable of reabsorption of HCO₃⁻ and selective secretion of H⁺, K⁺ and NH₃. Both proximal and distal tubules perform both reabsorption and secretion. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 22, Q161]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the pattern of inheritance for polygenic trait?
questions.explanation: Polygenic inheritance refers to the inheritance of a trait controlled by two or more genes. When human disorders are determined by mutation in the single gene then they are transmitted to the offspring as per Mendelian principle. Polygenic trait shows non-Mendelian inheritance pattern. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 22, Q162]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the seeds of cereals, the outer covering of endosperm separates the embryo by a protein-rich layer called:
questions.explanation: In monocot seeds, the outer covering of endosperm separates the embryo by a proteinous layer called aleurone layer. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 23, Q163]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following genetically engineered organisms was used by Eli Lilly to prepare human insulin?
questions.explanation: In 1983, Eli Lilly, an American company, prepared two DNA sequences corresponding to 'A' and 'B' chains of human insulin and introduced them in plasmids of E.coli (a gram negative bacterium) to produce insulin chains. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 23, Q165]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following are the post-transcriptional events in an eukaryotic cell?

options.A: Transport of pre-mRNA to cytoplasm prior to splicing.
options.B: Removal of introns and joining of exons.
options.C: Addition of methyl group at 5' end of hnRNA.
options.D: Addition of adenine residues at 3' end of hnRNA. E. Base pairing of two complementary RNAs.

questions.explanation: a. C, D, E only b. A, B, C only c. B, C, D only d. B, C, E only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which chromosome in the human genome has the highest number of genes?
questions.explanation: In human genome, Chromosome 1 has the highest number of genes, i.e., 2968. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 73, Q169]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What are the potential drawbacks in adoption of the IVF method?

options.D: Less adoption of orphans E. Not available in India F. Possibility that the early embryo does not survive

questions.explanation: a. A, B, C, E, F only b. B, D, F only c. A, C, D, F only d. A, B, C, D only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following is an example of ex-situ conservation?
questions.explanation: Zoological parks (Zoos), botanical gardens and wildlife safari parks are examples of ex-situ conservation. Sacred groves, biosphere reserves, national parks and wildlife sanctuaries are examples of in-situ conservation. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 74, Q171]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A specialised membranous structure in a prokaryotic cell which helps in cell wall formation, DNA replication and respiration is:
questions.explanation: Mesosome is membranous extension in bacterial cell that helps in cell wall formation, DNA replication and contains enzymes for respiration. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 74, Q172]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the above represented plasmid, an alien piece of DNA is inserted at EcoRI site. Which of the following strategies will be chosen to select the recombinant colonies?

options.A: Blue color colonies grown on ampicillin plates can be selected
options.B: Using ampicillin & tetracycline containing medium plate
options.C: Blue color colonies will be selected
options.D: White color colonies will be selected

questions.explanation: Since an alien piece of DNA is being inserted at EcoRI site, the gene β-galactosidase present here will undergo insertional inactivation. This gene is responsible for producing blue-colored colonies, but since it has been intentionally inactivated, white colored colonies will be produced. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 74, Q173]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the name of the blood vessel that carries deoxygenated blood from the body to the heart in a frog?
questions.explanation: Frog's heart is a muscular structure with three chambers. It receives deoxygenated blood from body parts through the major veins called vena cava. Vena cava carries deoxygenated blood. Aorta and pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 75, Q174]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of following organisms cannot fix nitrogen?
questions.explanation: a. E only b. A only c. D only d. B only

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: While trying to find out the characteristic of a newly found animal, a researcher did the histology of adult animal and observed a cavity with presence of mesodermal tissue towards the body wall but no mesodermal tissue was observed towards the alimentary canal. What could be the possible coelome of that animal?
questions.explanation: In pseudocoelomates, the body cavity is not entirely lined with mesoderm, instead, mesodermal tissue is present along the body wall but not towards the gut. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 75, Q176]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following statements refers to Reductionist Biology?

options.A: Behavioural approach to study and understand living organisms
options.B: Physico-chemical approach to study and understand living organisms
options.C: Physiological approach to study and understand living organisms
options.D: Chemical approach to study and understand living organisms

questions.explanation: The physico-chemical approach to study and understand living organisms is called 'Reductionist Biology'. [Source: NEET (UG)-2025 (Code-47) - Page 75, Q177]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Stomata of CAM plants:

options.A: open during the night and close during the day
options.D: open during the day and close at night

questions.explanation: CAM (Crassulacean Acid Metabolism) plants open stomata only at night (when temperature is low and humidity is high) to cause lesser loss of water (e.g. Agave, Opuntia, etc.). [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2003 - Page 128, Q09]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first step of photosynthesis is:

options.A: excitation of electron of chlorophyll by a photon of light
options.C: attachment of CO₂ to 5 carbon sugar

questions.explanation: The entire process of photosynthesis is driven by light energy coming from the sun. This energy is first captured by chlorophyll molecules and later on utilised for the synthesis of ATP molecules. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2000 - Page 128, Q10]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Pigment acting as a reaction centre during photosynthesis is:
questions.explanation: P700 is the reaction centre of photosystem I. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 128, Q12]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Nine-tenth of all photosynthesis of world (85-90%) is carried out by:

options.A: large trees with millions of branches and leaves
options.C: chlorophyll containing ferns of the forest

questions.explanation: 90% of total photosynthesis is carried out by aquatic plants, chiefly algae (80% in oceans and 10% in freshwater). 10% of total photosynthesis is performed by land plants. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 128, Q13]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Chlorophyll-a occurs in:
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll-a is a bluish green pigment, it is the primary photosynthetic pigment or universal photosynthetic pigment that occurs in all plants except photoautotrophic bacteria, i.e. found in all oxygenic photoautotrophs. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 128, Q15]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Photosynthetic pigments found in the chloroplasts occur in:
questions.explanation: Photosynthetic pigments are those pigments which occur on photosynthetic thylakoids of chloroplasts and absorb light energy for the purpose of photosynthesis. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1991 - Page 128, Q16]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In light reaction, plastoquinone facilitates the transfer of electrons from:
questions.explanation: In light reaction, plastoquinone facilitates the transfer of electrons from PS II to cytochrome b₆f complex. The process of light reaction starts with PS II (680 nm). [Source: NEET (Sep.) 2020 - Page 129, Q19]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In photosynthesis, the light-independent reactions take place at:
questions.explanation: The light-independent reactions (or dark reactions) take place in the stromal matrix of the chloroplasts. In light independent reactions, carbon dioxide is reduced to glucose. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2015 - Page 129, Q21]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Anoxygenic photosynthesis is characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Anoxygenic photosynthesis (in which O₂ is not released) is seen in Rhodospirillum which is a purple non-sulphur bacteria. It helps an organism to trap light energy and store it as chemical energy. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2014 - Page 129, Q22]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A process that makes important difference between C₃ and C₄ plants is:
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis is a light dependent process which occurs in C₃ plants. It is opposite to photosynthesis because during this process, uptake of O₂ and release of CO₂ take place. Due to the presence of Kranz anatomy, C₄ plants do not show photorespiration. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2012 - Page 129, Q23]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Oxygenic photosynthesis occurs in:
questions.explanation: Oscillatoria is a photosynthetic cyanobacterium. In this cyanobacteria during photosynthesis water is electron donor and oxygen is a byproduct, i.e., oxygenic photosynthesis occurs. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2009 - Page 129, Q24]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cyclic-photophosphorylation results in the formation of:
questions.explanation: Cyclic-photophosphorylation involves only pigment system-I and results in the formation of ATP only. No reduction of NADP to NADPH + H⁺ occurs. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2009 - Page 129, Q25]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first acceptor of electrons from an excited chlorophyll molecule of photosystem-II is:
questions.explanation: Plastoquinone is the first acceptor of electrons from an excited chlorophyll molecule of photosystem-II. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2007,08 - Page 130, Q26]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In photosystem-I the first electron acceptor is:
questions.explanation: In photosystem-I, the primary electron acceptor is probably a Fe-S protein. The reduced primary acceptor transfers the electrons to secondary electron acceptor. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2006 - Page 130, Q27]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which pigment system is inactivated in red drop?
questions.explanation: The fall in photosynthetic yield beyond red region of spectrum (680 nm) is called red drop. Reaction centre of PS-II is P680 while that of PS-I is P700. So in the red drop reaction PS-II is inactivated. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2001 - Page 130, Q29]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Of the total incident solar radiation the proportion of PAR is:
questions.explanation: PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation) designates the spectral range of solar radiation from 400-700 nm that photosynthetic organisms are able to use in the process of photosynthesis. Of the total incident solar radiation the proportion of PAR is less than 50%. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2011 - Page 127, Q03]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Stroma in the chloroplasts of higher plants contains:
questions.explanation: In higher plants, enzymes for light independent reactions (dark reactions) are present in the stroma of chloroplasts. Light dependent reaction occurs in grana of chloroplast. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2009 - Page 127, Q04]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Carbohydrates are commonly found as starch in plant storage organs. Which of the following five properties of starch (A-E) make it useful as a storage material?

options.D: osmotically inactive E. synthesised during photosynthesis

questions.explanation: a. B and C b. B and D c. A, C and E d. A and E

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Chlorophyll in chloroplasts is located in:
questions.explanation: The thylakoids of chloroplast are flattened vesicles arranged as a membranous network within the stroma. 50% of chloroplast proteins and various components involved in photosynthesis are present in thylakoid membranes. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2004 - Page 127, Q06]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which fractions of the visible spectrum of solar radiations are primarily absorbed by carotenoids of the higher plants?
questions.explanation: Carotenoids are a group of yellow, red and orange pigments which function as accessory pigments and protect chlorophyll molecules from destruction by intensive light rays. Carotenoids have three absorption peaks in the blue-violet range of the spectrum. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2003 - Page 128, Q07]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which element is located at the centre of the porphyrin ring in chlorophyll?
questions.explanation: Magnesium is at the centre of the porphyrin ring in chlorophyll. The general structure of chlorophyll was elucidated by Hand Fischer in 1940. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2003 - Page 128, Q08]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Chlorophyll-a molecule at its carbon atom 3 of the pyrrole ring-II has one of the following:
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll has a tetrapyrrole porphyrin head and a long chain alcohol called phytol tail. Chlorophyll-a has methyl group at carbon 3 of pyrrole ring and chlorophyll-b has formyl (aldehyde) group attached to this atom. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1996 - Page 128, Q11]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Maximum solar energy is trapped by:
questions.explanation: Maximum solar energy is trapped by growing grasses, as they have the largest surface area for absorption. Limited number of algal individual are growing in tank so, they absorb limited amount of light. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1993 - Page 128, Q14]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The size of chlorophyll molecule is:
questions.explanation: A chlorophyll molecule consists of two parts, the porphyrin ring (head) 15×15 Å and a phytol tail (20 Å). [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1988 - Page 128, Q17]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statement is incorrect?

options.A: Both ATP and NADPH + H⁺ are synthesised during non-cyclic photophosphorylation
options.B: Stroma lamellae have PS-I only and lack NADP reductase
options.C: Grana lamellae have both PS-I and PS-II
options.D: Cyclic photophosphorylation involves both PS-I and PS-II

questions.explanation: Only photosystem I is involved in cyclic photophosphorylation process. Cyclic photophosphorylation is a process in which an electron expelled by the excited photocentre is returned to it after passing through a series of electron carriers. [Source: NEET 2021 - Page 129, Q18]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following absorb light energy for photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll molecule absorbs light for photosynthesis. H₂O molecules provide H⁺ ions and electrons during photosynthesis. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2002 - Page 130, Q28]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Photochemical reactions in the chloroplast are directly involved in:

options.D: photolysis of water and phosphorylation of ADP to ATP

questions.explanation: CO₂ is fixed in the stroma of the chloroplast leading to the synthesis of PGA. All these do not require light. However, photolysis of water and phosphorylation of ADP to ATP requires light energy. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2000 - Page 130, Q30]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Protochlorophyll differs from chlorophyll in lacking:

options.A: 2 hydrogen atoms in one of its pyrrole rings
options.B: 2 hydrogen atoms in two of its pyrrole rings
options.C: 4 hydrogen atoms in one of its pyrrole rings
options.D: 4 hydrogen atoms in two of its pyrrole rings

questions.explanation: Protochlorophyll differs from chlorophyll in lacking 2 hydrogen atoms in one of its pyrrole rings. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1998 - Page 130, Q31]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: NADPH is generated through:
questions.explanation: NADPH is generated through photosystem-II. In non-cyclic photophosphorylation, protons released from photolysis and electrons emitted from P700 are ultimately passed on to NADP⁺ resulting in the formation of NADPH. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1997 - Page 130, Q32]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one occurs both during cyclic and non-cyclic modes of photophosphorylation?

options.A: involvement of both PS-I and PS-II

questions.explanation: In both, cyclic and non-cyclic photophosphorylation, formation of ATP takes place. Cyclic involves only PS-I, non-cyclic involves both PS-I and PS-II. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 130, Q33]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Formation of ATP in photosynthesis and respiration is an oxidation process which utilises the energy from:
questions.explanation: Cytochromes are the electron transport intermediates containing heme in which the iron undergoes valency changes during electron transfer and produces energy (ATP) in both photosynthesis and respiration. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 130, Q34]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Photosystem-II occurs in:
questions.explanation: PS-II is present in appressed part of granal thylakoids. PS-I is present in stroma thylakoids and non-appressed parts of granal thylakoids. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 130, Q35]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ferredoxin is a constituent of:
questions.explanation: Ferredoxin (Fd) is a soluble protein which acts as electron carrier and forms a constituent of PS-I. Ferredoxin passes electrons to reductase complex which helps in reducing NADP⁺ to NADPH. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1991 - Page 130, Q36]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: NADP is reduced to NADPH in:
questions.explanation: In photosynthesis during non-cyclic photophosphorylation involving both PS-I and PS-II, electrons released during photolysis of water are transferred to PS-II and then PS-I via a series of electron carriers. NADP⁺ combines with H⁺ with the help of NADP reductase to form NADPH. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1988 - Page 131, Q37]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first stable product of CO₂ fixation in Sorghum is:
questions.explanation: Most of the plants that are adapted to dry tropical regions form C-4 acid i.e. oxaloacetic acid as their first stable product. These plants are called C₄ plants. Sugarcane, maize, Sorghum, etc. are the examples of these plants. [Source: NEET 2021 - Page 131, Q38]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The oxygenation activity of RuBisCO enzyme in photorespiration leads to the formation of:

options.C: 1 molecule of 4-C compound and 1 molecule of 2-C compound

questions.explanation: The oxygenation activity of RuBisCO enzyme in photorespiration leads to the formation of 1 molecule of 3C compound (phosphoglycerate). In C₃ plants during oxygen fixation, one molecule of PGA(3C) and one molecule of 2-phosphoglycolate(2C) are formed. [Source: NEET (Sep.) 2020 - Page 131, Q40]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In Hatch and Slack pathway, the primary CO₂ acceptor is:
questions.explanation: In Hatch and Slack pathway, the primary CO₂ acceptor is phosphoenol pyruvate. This occurs in C₄ plants. Phosphoenol pyruvate, a 3-carbon compound, accepts CO₂ and forms oxaloacetic acid which is a 4-carbon compound. [Source: NEET (Odisha) 2019 - Page 131, Q41]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Phosphoenol Pyruvate (PEP) is the primary CO₂ acceptor in:
questions.explanation: Phosphoenol Pyruvate (PEP) is found in the mesophyll cell, which accepts the atmospheric CO₂ in C₄ plants and converts it to oxalo acetate - a C₄ compound. [Source: NEET 2017 - Page 131, Q42]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A plant in your garden avoids photorespiratory losses, has improved water use efficiency, shows high rates of photosynthesis at high temperatures and has improved efficiency of nitrogen utilisation. In which of the following physiological groups would you assign this plant?
questions.explanation: This plant is a C₄ plant as these group of plants shows little photorespiration, efficient in binding to CO₂ even at low concentrations, better utilisation of water as well as high rates of photosynthesis even at high temperatures. [Source: NEET 2016, Phase I - Page 131, Q43]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: PGA as the first CO₂ fixation product was discovered in photosynthesis of:
questions.explanation: The use of radioactive ¹⁴C by Melvin Calvin in algal (Chlorella) photosynthesis studies led to the discovery that the first CO₂ fixation product was a 3-carbon organic acid. The first product identified was 3-phosphoglyceric acid (PGA). [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2010 - Page 131, Q44]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: C₄ plants are more efficient in photosynthesis than C₃ plants due to:

options.B: presence of larger number of chloroplasts in the leaf cells

questions.explanation: C₄ plants are more efficient in photosynthesis than C₃ plants but use more energy. They possess the larger number of chloroplasts in the leaf cells, but the main reason for efficiency is lower rate of photorespiration. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2010,08 - Page 131, Q45]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the leaves of C₄ plants, malic acid formation during CO₂ fixation occurs in the cells of:
questions.explanation: The oxaloacetic acid is reduced to malic acid in mesophyll cells, from chloroplast of mesophyll cells the malic acid is transferred to the chloroplast of bundle sheath cells where it is decarboxylated to form CO₂ and pyruvic acid. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2007,08 - Page 132, Q46]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: As compared to a C₃ plant, how many additional molecules of ATP are needed for net production of one molecule of hexose sugar by C₄ plants?
questions.explanation: In C₃ plants every CO₂ molecule has to be fixed twice, so these plants are needed more energy for the synthesis of hexose sugar molecules than C₃ plants. 18 ATP molecules are required by C₃ plants for the synthesis of one molecule of hexose sugar while 30 ATP molecules are needed by the C₄ plants for the same. Thus, C₄ plants have a need of 12 ATP molecules extra. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2005 - Page 132, Q47]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Photosynthesis in C₄ plants is relatively less limited by atmospheric CO₂ levels because:

options.A: effective pumping of CO₂ into bundle sheath cells
options.B: RuBisCO in C₄ plants has higher affinity for CO₂
options.C: four carbon acids are the primary initial CO₂ fixation products
options.D: the primary fixation of CO₂ is mediated via PEP carboxylase

questions.explanation: The fixation of CO₂ in C₄ plants takes place in two places and by two different organic compounds. PEP is found in mesophyll cells which primarily fixes atmospheric CO₂ into oxalo acetic acid (4C). CO₂ is primarily fixed by PEP carboxylase because this enzyme has greater affinity to CO₂ than RuBisCO. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2005 - Page 132, Q48]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In C₃ plants, the first stable product of photosynthesis during the dark reaction is:
questions.explanation: In C₃ plants the first stable product formed during dark reaction is 3-phosphoglyceric acid. Since it is a 3 carbon compound hence, the pathway is referred as C₃ pathway. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2004 - Page 132, Q49]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In sugarcane plant ¹⁴CO₂ is fixed in malic acid, in which the enzyme that fixes CO₂ is:
questions.explanation: In C₄ plants, CO₂ is taken up by Phosphoenol-Pyruvate (PEP) and the reaction being catalysed by PEP carboxylase. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2003 - Page 132, Q50]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In photosynthesis energy from light reaction to dark reaction is transferred in the form of:
questions.explanation: As a result of light reaction, oxygen, NADPH and ATP are formed. Oxygen is released into the atmosphere while NADPH and ATP are utilised for reduction of CO₂ to carbohydrate in dark reaction. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2002 - Page 132, Q51]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which pair is wrong?

options.D: Hatch and Slack Pathway - Oxalo acetic acid

questions.explanation: Maize is a C₄ plant. C₄ plants have Kranz type anatomy of leaves. PGA is formed during Calvin cycle. OAA is formed during Hatch and Slack cycle. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2001 - Page 132, Q52]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: How many turns of Calvin cycle yield one molecule of glucose?
questions.explanation: Conversion of CO₂ to simple organic compounds is called CO₂ assimilation. Since one molecule of carbon is fixed in one turn of the Calvin cycle, six turns of the cycle are required to fix the glucose molecule containing 6 carbon atoms. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2000 - Page 132, Q53]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Fixation of one CO₂ molecule through Calvin cycle requires:
questions.explanation: 2 ATP are required during conversion of PGA to 1,3 diphosphoglyceric acid and 1 ATP is used during the conversion of ribulose phosphate to RuBP. Thus 3 ATP and 2 NADPH are required for fixing one CO₂ molecule. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2000 - Page 132, Q54]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following is represented by Calvin cycle?
questions.explanation: In dark phase or Calvin cycle, carbon dioxide is assimilated with the help of assimilatory power (ATP and NADPH) to produce organic acid. The cycle involves reduction of carbon involving carboxylation. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1996 - Page 133, Q55]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: C₄ cycle was discovered by:
questions.explanation: C₄ pathway or dicarboxylic acid pathway is an alternative path of CO₂ fixation in photosynthesis. It was discovered by MD Hatch and CR Slack in 1967, so also known as Hatch-Slack cycle. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 133, Q56]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The carbon dioxide acceptor in Calvin cycle in C₃ plants is:
questions.explanation: In C₃ plants, CO₂ combines with ribulose biphosphate (acceptor molecule) in the presence of RuBisCO and form two molecules of 3-Phosphoglyceric acid or PGA. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1993,95,96,99 - Page 133, Q57]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one is a C₄ plant?
questions.explanation: The plants in which the first stable product of dark reaction of photosynthesis is a 4-carbon compound are called C₄ plants, e.g. sugarcane, maize, sorghum, etc. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 133, Q58]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The enzyme that catalyses initial carbon dioxide fixation in C₄ plants is:
questions.explanation: In C₄ plants, mesophyll cells fix carbon dioxide with the help of phosphoenol-pyruvate (the first acceptor) in the presence of PEP carboxylase to a compound oxaloacetic acid (first product). [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992,2002 - Page 133, Q59]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Dark reactions of photosynthesis occur in:
questions.explanation: Light reaction of photosynthesis occurs in granal thylakoid membranes of chloroplasts while dark reaction occurs in the stroma or matrix, i.e. outside the photosynthetic lamellae of chloroplast. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1991 - Page 133, Q60]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which technique has helped in investigation of Calvin cycle?
questions.explanation: Calvin, Benson and Bassham utilized ¹⁴C (with long life) to trace the path of carbon in photosynthesis. Calvin was awarded Nobel Prize in 1961 for his work with ¹⁴C isotope. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1991 - Page 133, Q61]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Kranz anatomy is typical of:
questions.explanation: Leaves of C₄ plants (e.g. sugarcane, maize) are characterised by Kranz anatomy in which the mesophyll is undifferentiated and its cells occur in concentric layers around vascular bundles. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1990,95 - Page 133, Q62]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first carbon dioxide acceptor in C₄ plants is:
questions.explanation: In C₄ plants, phosphoenol-pyruvate is the first acceptor of CO₂ while ribulose biphosphate is the second acceptor. Oxalo Acetic Acid (OAA) is the first product of C₄ cycle. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1990,92 - Page 133, Q63]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In C₄ plants, Calvin cycle operates in:
questions.explanation: C₄ plants possess two types of chloroplasts: granal in mesophyll cells and agranal in bundle sheath cells. Mesophyll cells are specialised to perform light reaction and bundle sheath cells possess RuBisCO, here CO₂ is fixed through Calvin cycle. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1989 - Page 133, Q64]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A very efficient converter of solar energy with net productivity of 2-4 kg/m² or more is the crop of:
questions.explanation: In C₄ plants, (e.g. maize, sugarcane, sorghum) optimum temperature of photosynthesis is 30-45°C. Rate of net photosynthesis in full sunlight is higher than that of C₃ plants. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1989 - Page 133, Q65]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Carbon dioxide joins the photosynthetic pathway in:
questions.explanation: In dark reaction of photosynthesis, reducing agent (NADPH) and source of energy (ATP) formed during light reaction, are utilised in the conversion of CO₂ to carbohydrates. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1988 - Page 134, Q66]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During non-cyclic photophosphorylation, when electrons are lost from the reaction centre at PS-II, what is the source which replaces these electrons?
questions.explanation: During non-cyclic photophosphorylation, electrons expelled by the excited PS-II photocentre does not return to it. Therefore, it requires an external electron donor and that purpose is served by water. H₂O undergo photolysis and the electrons thus released are picked up by PS-II (P680). [Source: NEET (Oct.) 2020 - Page 134, Q67]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The process which makes major difference between C₃ and C₄ plants is:
questions.explanation: Photorespiration is the process which makes a difference between the C₃ and C₄ plants. In this process, there is a continuous loss of carbon fixed in the form of CO₂. [Source: NEET 2016, Phase II - Page 134, Q68]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The correct sequence of cell organelles during photorespiration is:
questions.explanation: Photorespiration requires three cell organelles in sequence of chloroplast, peroxisome and mitochondria. The first reaction occurs in stroma of chloroplast, then in peroxisome, and then in mitochondria. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2012 - Page 134, Q69]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During photorespiration, the oxygen consuming reaction(s) occur in:
questions.explanation: The first reaction of photorespiration occurs in stroma of chloroplast. In this reaction the RuBP consumes one oxygen molecule in presence of enzyme RuBisCO. In peroxisome the glycolate transferred from chloroplast takes up O₂ and formed the glyoxylate. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2006 - Page 134, Q70]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following is wrong in relation to photorespiration?

options.A: It is a characteristic of C₃ plants
options.C: It occurs in day time only
options.D: It is a characteristic of C₄ plants

questions.explanation: Dicker and Tio (1959) discovered photorespiration in tobacco plant. It is a light dependent process of oxygenation of RuBP. C₄ plants avoid photorespiration by following Hatch Slack pathway. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2003 - Page 134, Q71]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which enzyme is most abundantly found on earth?
questions.explanation: RuBisCO (RuBP carboxylase) is the most abundant protein on this planet. RuBisCO constitutes 16% of chloroplast protein. It is required for CO₂ fixation with RuBP in Calvin cycle. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1999 - Page 134, Q72]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Photorespiration is favoured by:
questions.explanation: Photorespiration is light induced oxidation of photosynthetic intermediates with the help of oxygen. It is stimulated by high O₂ concentration or low CO₂, high light intensity, high temperature and ageing of leaf. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1996 - Page 134, Q73]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The substrate for photorespiration is:
questions.explanation: Photorespiration is the oxidation of photosynthetic intermediate without production of CO₂, ATP and NADH₂. The substrate for photorespiration is a 2-carbon compound glycolic acid (glycolate). [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1989 - Page 134, Q74]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: With reference to factors affecting the rate of photosynthesis, which of the following statements is not correct?

options.A: Light saturation for CO₂ fixation occurs at 10% of full sunlight
options.B: Increasing atmospheric CO₂ concentration up to 0.05% can enhance CO₂ fixation rate
options.C: C₃ plants respond to higher temperature with enhanced photosynthesis, while C₄ plants have much lower temperature optimum
options.D: Tomato is a greenhouse crop, which can be grown in CO₂ enriched atmosphere for higher yield

questions.explanation: In C₄ plants, the initial fixation of CO₂ occurs in mesophyll cells. RuBisCO can work as both carboxylase and oxygenase. At higher temperature, its affinity for CO₂ decrease and it works as oxygenase. Therefore, at higher temperature photosynthesis decrease in C₃ plants, while in C₄ plants it increases. [Source: NEET 2017 - Page 134, Q75]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The oxygen evolved during photosynthesis comes from water molecules. Which one of the following pairs of elements involved in this reaction?
questions.explanation: Photolysis of water during photosynthesis evolve nascent oxygen in the presence of manganese, calcium and chloride ions. [Source: NEET 2016, Phase I - Page 135, Q77]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Plants adapted to low light intensity have:

options.A: larger photosynthetic unit size than the sun plants
options.B: higher rate of CO₂ fixation than the sun plants

questions.explanation: Shade tolerant plants have lower photosynthetic rates and hence, lower growth rates. On the other hand, these plants have larger photosynthetic unit size than the sun plants. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2004 - Page 135, Q78]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The principle of limiting factors was proposed by:
questions.explanation: The principle of limiting factors was given by Blackmann, a British plant physiologist in 1905, according to him, light intensity, carbon dioxide concentration and temperature are the limiting factors in photosynthesis. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1996 - Page 135, Q79]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Photosynthetically active radiation is represented by the range of wavelength:
questions.explanation: Photosynthetically Active Region (PAR) of solar radiation is visible region. It consists of radiations having wavelength between 400 to 700 nm. Green plants use this wavelength in the process of manufacture of food. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1996,2004,05 - Page 135, Q80]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A photosynthesis plant is releasing ¹⁸O more than the normal. The plant must have been supplied with:
questions.explanation: Ruben, Hassid and Kamen (1941) using heavy isotope of oxygen, O¹⁸ in water, found that oxygen evolved in photosynthesis comes from water. Evolution of oxygen does not require carbon dioxide. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1993 - Page 135, Q81]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: At a temperature above 35°C:

options.A: rate of photosynthesis will decline earlier than that of respiration
options.B: rate of respiration will decline earlier than that of photosynthesis

questions.explanation: Optimum temperature for photosynthesis is 10°-25°C for C₃ plants and 30°-45°C for C₄ plants. Optimum temperature for respiration is 20°C-30°C, i.e. respiration has a higher temperature optimum than the photosynthesis and thus declines later. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 135, Q82]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During monsoon, the rice crop of Eastern states of India shows lesser yield due to limiting factor of:
questions.explanation: According to the principle of limiting factor, the rate of the process is limited by the pace of the slowest factor. Light intensity varies with latitude, altitude, season, topography. In Eastern states, low light intensity during monsoon results in low photosynthesis and hence, lesser yield. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1991 - Page 135, Q83]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Conversion of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate, the first irreversible reaction of glycolysis, is catalysed by:
questions.explanation: Conversion of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate during glycolysis is catalysed by the enzyme hexokinase. During this step, glucose is phosphorylated to glucose-6-phosphate by ATP. [Source: NEET (National) 2019 - Page 136, Q01]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following biomolecules is common to respiration-mediated breakdown of fats, carbohydrates and proteins?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates, fats and proteins, all can be used as a substrate in cellular respiration. All of them first get converted to acetyl Co-A to enter into Krebs cycle of aerobic cellular respiration. [Source: NEET 2016, Phase II - Page 136, Q02]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In glycolysis, during oxidation electrons are removed by:
questions.explanation: When 3-phosphoglyceraldehyde is converted into 1,3 diphosphoglyceric acid, two electrons and two protons are released which are utilised to convert NAD⁺ to NADH and H⁺. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2004 - Page 136, Q03]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In animal cells, the first stage of glucose breakdown is:
questions.explanation: Glycolysis refers to the sequence of reactions by which glucose is degraded anaerobically into pyruvic acid inside cytoplasm. The net gain of molecules of ATP during glycolysis is 2. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 136, Q04]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: End product of glycolysis is:
questions.explanation: During glycolysis one molecule of glucose is broken down into two molecules of pyruvic acid in the cytoplasm. Glycolysis is the common path of aerobic and anaerobic respiration. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1990 - Page 136, Q05]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: EMP can produce a total of:
questions.explanation: EMP (Embden-Meyerhof Parnas Pathway) refers to glycolysis, in which one molecule of glucose is broken down to two molecules of pyruvic acid. In this process there is a gain of 2NADH (2×3 ATP) and 2 ATP, i.e. total 8 ATP. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1990 - Page 136, Q06]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Incomplete oxidation of glucose into pyruvic acid with several intermediate steps is known as:
questions.explanation: Glycolysis is the sequence of enzyme mediated reactions by which glucose is degraded anaerobically into pyruvic acid in cell cytoplasm. The net gain of molecules of ATP during glycolysis is two. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1988 - Page 136, Q07]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Pyruvate dehydrogenase activity during aerobic respiration requires:
questions.explanation: Pyruvate dehydrogenase enzyme is involved in the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl Co-A, after the completion of glycolysis and before the start of Krebs' cycle. This enzyme is made up of decarboxylase, lipoic acid, transacetylase and Mg²⁺ ion. Mg²⁺ acts as a cofactor. [Source: NEET (Oct.) 2020 - Page 137, Q09]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The number of substrate level phosphorylation in one turn of citric acid cycle is:
questions.explanation: The number of substrate level phosphorylation in one turn of citric acid cycle is 1. During Krebs' or citric acid cycle, succinyl-CoA is acted upon by enzyme succinyl-CoA synthetase to form succinate. The reaction releases sufficient energy to form ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation. [Source: NEET (Sep.) 2020 - Page 137, Q10]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where is respiratory Electron Transport System (ETS) located in plants?
questions.explanation: Respiratory Electron Transport System (ETS) in plants is located in inner mitochondrial membrane. It serves as the site of oxidative phosphorylation through the action of ATP synthase. [Source: NEET (Odisha) 2019 - Page 137, Q11]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of these statements is incorrect?

options.A: Glycolysis operates as long as it is supplied with NAD that can pick up hydrogen atoms
options.C: Enzymes of TCA cycle are present in mitochondrial matrix
options.D: Oxidative phosphorylation takes place in outer mitochondrial membrane

questions.explanation: Oxidative phosphorylation is the process of ATP formation due to the transfer of electrons from NADH or FADH₂ to oxygen molecule by a series of electron carriers. This process occurs in the inner mitochondrial membrane because of its less permeability, presence of ETC proteins and ATP synthase. [Source: NEET 2018 - Page 137, Q12]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the role of NAD⁺ in cellular respiration?

options.A: It is a nucleotide source of ATP synthesis
options.B: It functions as an electron carrier
options.D: It is the final electron acceptor for anaerobic respiration

questions.explanation: NAD⁺ functions as an electron carrier in cellular respiration. NAD is an oxidising agent which accepts electrons and then transfer them to the Electron Transport System (ETS). [Source: NEET 2018 - Page 137, Q13]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which statement is wrong for Krebs' cycle?

options.A: There are three points in the cycle where NAD⁺ is reduced to NADH + H⁺
options.B: There is one point in the cycle where FAD⁺ is reduced to FADH₂
options.C: During conversion of succinyl Co-A to succinic acid, a molecule of GTP is synthesised
options.D: The cycle starts with condensation of acetyl group (acetyl Co-A) with pyruvic acid to yield citric acid

questions.explanation: Krebs' cycle starts with the condensation of acetyl group with oxaloacetic acid and water to yield citric acid. During conversion of succinic acid to fumaric acid FAD⁺ is reduced to FADH₂. [Source: NEET 2017 - Page 137, Q14]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Oxidative phosphorylation is:

options.A: formation of ATP by transfer of phosphate group from a substrate to ADP
options.B: oxidation of phosphate group in ATP
options.C: addition of phosphate group to ATP
options.D: formation of ATP by energy released from electrons removed during substrate oxidation

questions.explanation: Oxidative phosphorylation is the process of formation of ATP from ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi) in the presence of oxygen. It occurs mainly in the Electron Transport Chain (ETC) of cellular respiration. It involves formation of ATP by energy released from electrons removed during substrate oxidation. [Source: NEET 2016, Phase II - Page 137, Q15]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the metabolites is common to respiration mediated breakdown of fats, carbohydrates and proteins?
questions.explanation: Acetyl Co-A is common to respiration mediated breakdown of fats, carbohydrates and proteins. Glucose and fructose are phosphorylated to give rise to glucose-6-phosphate. Pyruvic acid is the end product of glycolysis. [Source: NEET 2013 - Page 137, Q16]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Aerobic respiratory pathway is appropriately termed:
questions.explanation: An amphibolic pathway is a biochemical pathway that serves both anabolic and catabolic processes. An important example of an amphibolic pathway is the Krebs' cycle, which involves both the catabolism of carbohydrates and fatty acid and the synthesis of anabolic precursors. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2009 - Page 138, Q18]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The chemiosmotic coupling hypothesis of oxidative phosphorylation proposes that Adenosine Triphosphate (ATP) is formed because:

options.A: high energy bonds are formed in mitochondrial proteins
options.B: ADP is pumped out of the matrix into the intermembrane space
options.C: a proton gradient forms across the inner membrane
options.D: there is a change in the permeability of the inner mitochondrial membrane toward ADP

questions.explanation: Peter Mitchell (1961) gave a hypothesis known as chemiosmotic hypothesis for ATP synthesis. When electrons flow from dual proton, electron carrier to a non-hydrogen carrier the H⁺ are released and expelled into the intermembrane space and thus creates a proton gradient. Due to the proton motive force the protons flow back and energy liberated activates ATPase to synthesize ATP. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2008 - Page 138, Q19]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The overall goal of glycolysis, Krebs' cycle and the electron transport system is the formation of:

options.B: ATP in one large oxidation reaction

questions.explanation: Glycolysis, Krebs' cycle and electron transport system are meant for ATP synthesis in different steps. ATP is the energy currency of cell. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2007 - Page 138, Q20]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: All enzymes of TCA cycle are located in the mitochondrial matrix except one which is located in inner mitochondrial membranes in eukaryotes and in cytosol in prokaryotes. This enzyme is:
questions.explanation: Succinate dehydrogenase enzyme is present on inner membrane of mitochondria and catalyses the oxidation of succinate to fumarate. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2007 - Page 138, Q21]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During which stage, in the complete oxidation of glucose are the greatest number of ATP molecules formed from ADP?

options.C: conversion of pyruvic acid to acetyl Co-A

questions.explanation: The last step of aerobic respiration is the oxidation of reduced coenzymes, i.e., NADH and FADH₂ by molecular oxygen. By oxidation of 1 molecule of NADH, 3 ATP molecules are produced and by oxidation of 1 molecule of FADH₂, 2 ATP molecules are produced. The greatest number of ATP molecules are produced in the electron transport chain. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2005 - Page 138, Q22]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Chemiosmotic theory of ATP synthesis in the chloroplast and mitochondria is based on:
questions.explanation: Chemiosmotic hypothesis for oxidative phosphorylation (ATP synthesis) was proposed by Peter Mitchell in 1961. This theory is based on proton gradient. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2005 - Page 138, Q23]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following concerns photophosphorylation?
questions.explanation: Phosphorylation refers to the process in which ATP is made when energy is used to bind another phosphate to ADP. Photophosphorylation reactions are part of both respiration and photosynthesis. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2003 - Page 138, Q24]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which one of the following do the two names refer to one and the same thing?

options.A: Tricarboxylic acid cycle and urea cycle
options.C: Tricarboxylic acid cycle and citric acid cycle
options.D: Citric acid cycle and Calvin cycle

questions.explanation: Tricarboxylic acid cycle is also known as citric acid cycle. This is an aerobic process, that takes place in the matrix of mitochondria. Krebs that discovered this cycle in 1937. So, this is also known as Krebs' cycle. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2003 - Page 139, Q25]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The mechanism of ATP formation both in chloroplast and mitochondria is explained by:
questions.explanation: In chemiosmotic-coupling hypothesis, outward pumping of protons across the inner chloroplast or mitochondrial membrane results in accumulation of protons between outer membrane and inner membrane. A proton gradient is thus established. As protons now flow back passively down the gradient, the proton motive force is utilised to synthesise ATP. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1997 - Page 139, Q26]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In Krebs cycle FAD participates as electron acceptor during the conversion of:
questions.explanation: Electrons and H- ions during oxidation of succinic acid to fumaric acid, are taken up by FAD which gets reduced to FADH₂. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1997 - Page 139, Q27]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Oxidative phosphorylation involves simultaneous oxidation and phosphorylation to finally form:
questions.explanation: In oxidative phosphorylation ATP is formed as the electrons are transferred from NADH or FADH₂ to O₂ by a series of electron carriers, located in the inner membrane of mitochondria. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1996 - Page 139, Q28]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Krebs cycle occurs in:
questions.explanation: Krebs cycle occurs inside the matrix of mitochondria. The cycle is also named as citric acid cycle or tricarboxylic acid cycle. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1996 - Page 139, Q29]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is essential for conversion of pyruvic acid into acetyl Co-A?
questions.explanation: The oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate into acetyl Co-A involves the presence of at least five essential co-factors and an enzyme complex. The co-factors involved are Mg ions, Thiamine Pyrophosphate (TPP), NAD⁺, Coenzyme-A (Co-A) and lipoic acid. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1995 - Page 139, Q30]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: ATP is injected in cyanide poisoning because it is:

options.C: Na⁺-K⁺ pump operates at the cell membranes

questions.explanation: Cyanide is a deadly poison. It stops respiration by inhibiting electron flow from cyt-b to cyt-c. ATP is the energy currency of cell is injected in cyanide poisoning because it is necessary for cellular functions. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 139, Q31]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Out of 38 ATP molecules produced per glucose, 32 ATP molecules are formed from NADH/FADH₂ in:
questions.explanation: Respiratory chain helps in forming 32 ATP molecules from NADH/FADH₂ molecules. Oxidative phosphorylation is the synthesis of energy rich ATP molecules with the help of energy liberated during oxidation of reduced coenzymes produced in glycolysis and Krebs cycle. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1993 - Page 139, Q32]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: End product of citric acid/Krebs cycle is:
questions.explanation: Krebs' cycle or citric acid cycle that takes place in the matrix of mitochondrion begins by linking acetyl Co-A to oxaloacetic acid forming citric acid. In the presence of various enzymes, cycle continues through the formation of various intermediates and release of CO₂ and H₂O as end-products. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1993 - Page 139, Q33]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Link between glycolysis, Krebs' cycle and β-oxidation of fatty acid or carbohydrate and fat metabolism is:
questions.explanation: The pyruvic acid synthesised from glycolysis enters into mitochondria and undergoes oxidative decarboxylation to produce CO₂ and NADH₂. The product combines with coenzyme-A to form acetyl Co-A. It is the connecting link between glycolysis, Krebs' cycle and fat oxidation. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992,90 - Page 139, Q34]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Oxidative phosphorylation is production of:
questions.explanation: Oxidative phosphorylation is the synthesis of ATP from ADP and inorganic phosphate which occurs with the help of energy obtained from oxidation of reduced coenzymes formed in cellular respiration. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 139, Q35]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Terminal cytochrome of respiratory chain which donates electrons to oxygen is:
questions.explanation: The ETS system contains various electron carriers such as cytochrome. The correct sequence is cyt-b, cyt-c, cyt-c₁, cyt-a, cyt-a₃. cyt-a₃ is the terminal cytochrome, it possesses two copper centers, which help in transfer of electron to oxygen. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 140, Q36]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: NADP⁺ is reduced to NADPH in:
questions.explanation: Pentose Phosphate Pathway (PPP) or Hexose Monophosphate Shunt (HMP) occurs in the cytosol of mammalian cells. It involves oxidation of glucose to CO₂ and water through a series of reactions in which NADP is reduced to NADPH. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1988 - Page 140, Q37]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Respiratory Quotient (RQ) value of tripalmitin is:
questions.explanation: The RQ value of tripalmitin is 0.7. It can be calculated as: 2 C₅₁H₉₈O₆ + 145 O₂ → 102 CO₂ + 98 H₂O. RQ = 102 CO₂/145 O₂ = 0.7. RQ of common fats is usually less than 1 under aerobic conditions. [Source: NEET (National) 2019 - Page 140, Q38]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: How many ATP molecules could maximally be generated from one molecule of glucose, if the complete oxidation of one mole of glucose to CO₂ and H₂O yields 686 kcal and the useful chemical energy available in the high energy phosphate bond of one mole of ATP is 12 kcal?
questions.explanation: One mole of ATP liberates 12 kcal of energy, so 686 kcal will be liberated by 686/12 = 57.1 ATP molecules. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2006 - Page 140, Q39]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: How many ATP molecules are produced by aerobic oxidation of one molecule of glucose?
questions.explanation: A total of 38 molecules of ATP are produced during aerobic respiration of one molecule of glucose: 8 ATP from glycolysis, 6 ATP from acetyl Co-A, 24 ATP from Krebs cycle. Total = 38 ATP. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2002 - Page 140, Q40]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Net gain of ATP molecules during aerobic respiration is:
questions.explanation: During aerobic respiration, 38 ATP molecules are gained. If specifically aerobic respiration in eukaryote is asked, then the answer would be 36 ATP because 2 ATP molecules are produced by FADH₂ which accepts the H⁺ from 2 NADH molecules produced in glycolysis. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1999 - Page 140, Q41]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Respiratory quotient (RQ) for fatty acid is:
questions.explanation: Respiratory Quotient (RQ) = Volume of CO₂ formed / Volume of O₂ utilised. In fats, large amount of O₂ is used to combine with H₂ so output of CO₂ is less and RQ is only 0.70, i.e., less than unity. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1995 - Page 140, Q42]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Respiratory substrate yielding maximum number of ATP molecule is:
questions.explanation: Respiratory substrate yielding maximum number of ATP molecule is glucose. One glucose molecule on aerobic respiration yields 36 ATP molecules. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 140, Q43]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Maximum amount of energy/ATP is liberated on oxidation of:
questions.explanation: Fats or lipids are second to carbohydrates as a source of energy. By weight, each gram of fat yields about 9.3 kcal of energy, i.e. more than double of that yielded by glucose. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 140, Q44]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Apparatus to measure rate of respiration and RQ is:
questions.explanation: Respirometer is an instrument used to measure the rate of respiration and also Respiratory Quotient (RQ). The most common respirometer is Ganong's respirometer. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 140, Q45]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When one glucose molecule is completely oxidised, it changes:
questions.explanation: In aerobic respiration or biological oxidation of one glucose molecule, 38 ADP molecules change into 38 ATP molecules, where donor phosphate is inorganic phosphate. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1992 - Page 140, Q46]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following statements about cytochrome P450 is wrong?

options.C: It has an important role in metabolism
options.D: It is an enzyme involved in oxidation reactions

questions.explanation: Cytochrome is not a coloured cell, instead this is a respiratory pigment - mixture of iron and protein which are electron acceptors. Cytochrome are membrane bound heme proteins containing heme groups. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1999 - Page 141, Q47]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: RQ is:
questions.explanation: Respiratory Quotient (RQ) is the ratio of volume of CO₂ evolved to the volume of oxygen consumed per unit time per unit weight. Therefore, RQ = CO₂/O₂. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1988 - Page 141, Q48]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which one of the following processes CO₂ is not released?
questions.explanation: Lactic acid fermentation is process by which glucose, fructose and sucrose are converted into energy and the metabolite lactate. It is an anaerobic fermentation reaction. CO₂ is not released in lactate fermentation. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2014 - Page 141, Q49]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The energy-releasing metabolic process in which substrate is oxidised without an external electron acceptor is called:
questions.explanation: Fermentation takes place in the lack of oxygen (when the electron transport chain is unusable) and becomes the cell's primary means of ATP production. It turns NADH and pyruvate from glycolysis into NAD⁺. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2010,08 - Page 141, Q50]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In alcoholic fermentation:

options.B: triose phosphate is the electron donor while acetaldehyde is the electron acceptor
options.C: triose phosphate is the electron donor while pyruvic acid is the electron acceptor

questions.explanation: In alcoholic fermentation, NADH (formed during conversion of triose-3-phosphate to 3 phosphoglycerate) is oxidised to NAD⁺ and electrons are accepted by acetaldehyde formed by decarboxylation of pyruvate. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2003 - Page 141, Q51]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Fermentation is anaerobic production of:

options.B: alcohol, lactic acid or similar compounds

questions.explanation: Fermentation is defined as anaerobic break down of carbohydrates and other organic compounds to form aldehyde, alcohol and organic acids (lactic acid) with the help of microorganisms or their enzymes. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1996 - Page 141, Q52]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Fermentation products of yeast are:
questions.explanation: Yeast cells undergo alcoholic fermentation in which glucose is first converted into pyruvic acid. In the presence of pyruvic decarboxylase, it is changed into acetaldehyde. Alcohol dehydrogenase changes it to ethyl alcohol and CO₂. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1994 - Page 141, Q53]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Life without air would be:
questions.explanation: Anaerobic means in the absence of molecular oxygen, so life without air would be anaerobic. The atmosphere of earth at the time of origin of life was without free oxygen atoms, so the primitive atmosphere was reducing. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1993 - Page 141, Q54]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Out of 36 ATP molecules produced per glucose molecule during respiration:

options.A: 2 are produced outside glycolysis and 34 during respiratory chain
options.B: 2 are produced outside mitochondria and 34 inside mitochondria
options.C: 2 during glycolysis and 34 during Krebs cycle

questions.explanation: A total of 38 ATP molecules are produced per glucose molecule during respiration. Out of which, 2 ATP are produced outside mitochondria (i.e. glycolysis in cytoplasm) and 36 ATP inside mitochondria (i.e. 2 ATP through Krebs cycle and 34 ATP from NADH/FADH₂ through respiratory chain). [Source: CBSE AIPMT 1991 - Page 141, Q55]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The plant hormone used to destroy weeds in a field is:
questions.explanation: 2,4-D hormone is used as a herbicide to destroy weeds. Auxins like IAA and IBA are used to induce parthenocarpy. [Source: NEET 2021 - Page 142, Q01]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Plants follow different pathways in response to environment or phases of life to form different kinds of structures. This ability is called:
questions.explanation: Plant plasticity refers to the ability to modify itself by forming different kind of structures to adapt and cope with changes in its environment. It can be intrinsic plasticity or extrinsic plasticity. [Source: NEET 2021 - Page 142, Q02]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Inhibitor substances in dormant seeds cannot be removed by subjecting seeds to:
questions.explanation: Presence of inhibitory substances in dormant seeds can be removed by subjecting seeds to growth hormones like gibberellic acid, cytokinins, by stratification, and low concentration application of nitrates. Ascorbic acid is known to cause seed dormancy as it is a potent chemical inhibitor. [Source: NEET (Oct.) 2020 - Page 142, Q03]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A species which was introduced for ornamentation but has become a troublesome weed in India:
questions.explanation: Eichhornia crassipes (water hyacinth) was introduced in several tropical countries including India for ornamentation but later this exotic species became a troublesome aquatic weed. This free floating weed clogged rivers and lakes and threatened the survival of many native species. [Source: NEET (Oct.) 2020 - Page 142, Q05]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is not an inhibitory substance governing seed dormancy?
questions.explanation: Gibberellic acid is not an inhibitory substance governing seed dormancy because gibberellic acid promotes growth and elongation of cells. Action of ABA is counteracted by GA, which promotes seed germination at appropriate time. [Source: NEET (Sep.) 2020 - Page 143, Q06]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The process of growth is maximum during:
questions.explanation: The process of growth is maximum during log phase (exponential phase) because during log phase the growth rate of the cells gradually increases, at a maximum rate. In exponential growth, the initial growth is slow (lag phase) and thereafter it increases rapidly. [Source: NEET (Sep.) 2020 - Page 143, Q07]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Name the plant growth regulator which upon spraying on sugarcane crop, increases the length of stem, thus increasing the yield of sugarcane crop:
questions.explanation: Spraying on sugarcane crop with gibberellins increases the length of the stem, thus increasing the yield by as much as 20 tonnes per acre. [Source: NEET (Sep.) 2020 - Page 143, Q08]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Removal of shoot tips is very useful technique to boost the production of tea leaves. This is because:

options.A: gibberellins prevent bolting and are inactivated
options.B: auxins prevent leaf drop at early stages
options.C: effect of auxins is removed and growth of lateral buds is enhanced

questions.explanation: Removal of shoot tips is a very useful technique to boost the production of tea leaves. This is because effect of auxin is removed and growth of lateral bud is enhanced. This phenomenon in most higher plants in which growing apical buds inhibit growth of lateral buds due to effects of auxin is called apical dominance. [Source: NEET (Odisha) 2019 - Page 143, Q09]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: It takes very long time for pineapple plants to produce flowers. Which combination of hormones can be applied to artificially induce flowering in pineapple plants throughout the year to increase yield?
questions.explanation: Auxin and ethylene can be applied to artificially induce flowering in pineapple plants throughout the year to increase yield. Auxin induces flowering in pineapple and ethylene helps to synchronise flower and fruit growth in this plant. [Source: NEET (National) 2019 - Page 143, Q11]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Fruit and leaf drop at early stages can be prevented by the application of:
questions.explanation: Auxin delay abscission of leaves and fruits at early stages. Whenever leaf or fruit fall occurs, the organ concerned stops producing auxin. However, it promotes abscission of older, mature leaves and fruits. [Source: NEET 2017 - Page 143, Q12]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: You are given a tissue with its potential for differentiation in an artificial culture. Which of the following pairs of hormones would you add to the medium to secure shoots as well as roots?
questions.explanation: When a tissue with a potential of differentiation is grown in an artificial medium containing auxin and cytokinin in a specific ratio, it starts differentiating. Auxin initiate root formation while cytokinin starts shoot formation. [Source: NEET 2016, Phase II - Page 143, Q14]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Auxin can be bioassayed by:
questions.explanation: Auxin is a phytohormone that is often bioassayed by Avena coleoptile curvature test. The angle of curvature of a decapitated oat coleoptile is measured after placing an agar block containing auxin on one side. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2015 - Page 143, Q15]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Dr. F. Went noted that if coleoptile tips were removed and placed on agar for one hour, the agar would produce a bending when placed on one side of freshly cut coleoptile stumps. Of what significance is this experiment?

options.A: It made possible the isolation and exact identification of auxin
options.B: It is the basis for quantitative determination of small amounts of growth-promoting substances
options.C: It supports the hypothesis that IAA is auxin
options.D: It demonstrated polar movement of auxins

questions.explanation: Dr. F. Went isolated auxin from Avena coleoptile tip. His experiment demonstrated the polar movement of auxins, i.e., it showed that the plants grow towards light in response to a signal generated in the tip of coleoptile by a plant hormone auxin. It is the basis for quantitative determination of small amounts of growth-promoting substances. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2014 - Page 144, Q16]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Senescence as an active developmental cellular process in the growth and functioning of a flowering plant, is indicated in:
questions.explanation: Abscission is natural shedding of leaves, foliage branches, fruits, floral parts, etc. According to Leopold (1967) abscission is a senescence phenomenon. Senescence is known as the sum of deteriorative processes which naturally terminate the functional life of an organism. [Source: CBSE AIPMT 2008 - Page 144, Q17]

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Some of the growth regulators affect stomatal opening. Closure of stomata is brought about by:
